The tabernacle had three major sections (see diagram on page 60)。 The first section, the outer court, had the first altar for the offering of the sacrifices. This points to our experience of Christ as our redemption. The altar was covered with bronze taken from the censers of those who rebelled against Moses (Num. 16:38-40). At the bronze altar, in figure, Christ was judged for our rebellion that we might be redeemed. The outer court also had the laver. The top of the laver was covered with bronze mirrors from the serving women of the congregation (Exo. 38:8). When we go to the laver, we get an honest reflection of our need to wash in Christ as the Spirit. In our experience we come to the first altar where Christ is our redemption and then proceed to the laver, which is Christ as the washing and cleansing Spirit.
Next we enter the Holy Place. The first item of furniture in the Holy Place is the showbread table, a table displaying loaves of bread, signifying Christ as the bread of life (John 6:33, 35), The next item is the lampstand. In our experience the light comes out of life, so we go from the showbread table to the lampstand, where we experience Christ as the light of life (8:12), From there we go to the golden incense altar, the second altar, for the offering of the incense. The incense altar typifies Christ Himself in His prayer and in His intercession (Rev. 8:3). The incense altar does not typify Christ's prayer directly, but Christ's person in His prayer life of intercession. The incense altar is Christ through whom we are ushered into the Holy of Holies. The dotted line in the diagram indicates the separating veil, which typifies the flesh, of Christ, torn and rent on the cross (Matt, 27:51).
In previous messages we mentioned that the Holy of Holies was a reality, even in the Old Testament, because the glory of God, the speaking of God, and the presence of God. were there. The ark in the Holy of Holies typifies Christ as the embodiment of God for His testimony In. the Holy Place we experience something of Christ, but in the Holy of Holies we experience Christ Himself； personally and directly.
EXPERIENCING CHRIST IN THE OUTER COURT, IN THE HOLY PLACE, AND IN THE HOLY OF HOLIES
The book of Hebrews reveals that we may experience Christ at the altar and at the laver in the outer court in an outward way, at the show-bread table, at the lampstand, and at the incense altar in the Holy Place in an inner way, and at the ark of the testimony and in the ark of the testimony in the Holy of Holies in the innermost way (Heb. 9:2-4). "We are on a journey inward, into the depths of God and into the depths of our God-constituted and "zoe-fied" being so that we may enter deeply into the Spirit. We enter though Christ as a heavenly ladder into the divine and mystical realm to enter into God's being to receive His infusion5 to enjoy Him as everything, and to be one with Him to pray according t.o what is in His being. We need to be brought onward by the Lord s shepherding. The goal is the ark and the inner contents of the ark. Then the ark experientially becomes the interceding Christ. In such an experience, we pray not for our spirituality, nor for our problems, situation5 and need, but for God's interests. We forget ourselves and enter into God and join with the interceding Christ to carry out God's administration.
The Lord's recovery needs intercessors. We have to realize as we advance in our experience that we not only go higher, but we must go deeper as well. We need to overcome by the law of life the shallowness and superficiality of our American culture and the tendency to have merely an outward church life. It would be marvelous if thousands of brothers and sisters in the Lord s recovery could enjoy the fact that they have the right and privilege to enter into the Holy of Holies and would pray hidden prayers, observed by the Father who is in secret, to be rewarded at the judgment seat of Christ.
In the book of Acts the apostles said, "We will continue steadfastly in prayer and in the ministry of the word" (6:4). The heavenly Christ is interceding and ministering. What then do the apostles do? They pray; that is, they are one with the heavenly Christ praying. Then they minister the word. Their ministry of the word is the practical expression of the ministering of the heavenly Christ. Prayer determines and decides how much ministry there will be. If the prayer is great, prevailing, powerful, and with authority, there will be a release of the word and of grace, and there will be blessing beyond what we ever hoped to see. It depends upon how deep we will go into the innermost experiences. I believe this has a lot to do with vital groups praying to release God's move on the earth.
EXPERIENCING THE PROCESSED AND CONSUMMATED TRIUNE GOD IN THE ARK IN THE HOLY OF HOLIES
In the ark in the Holy of Holies, we experience the processed and consummated Triune God一the Father in His divine nature as the source of all supply, Christ as the hidden manna and the resurrection life, and the Spirit of life as the inner law of life.
The Golden Pot Signifying the Nature of God the Father as the Unique Source
The golden pot signifies the divine nature, the nature of God the Father, who is the unique source (Heb. 9:4b; 2 Pet. 1:4; Matt. 6:4, 6, 18; 1 Cor. 8:6a; Eph. 3:14-15). We must realize that there is only one legitimate source in the universe. None of us has any right or standing to initiate anything out from ourselves. The source determines the outcome. If the source of a certain action or relationship is God the Father, we can be assured— if we are one with Him in the process as well一that the outcome will be excellent for us and for the church. However, if the source is wrong, it must be terminated. The Lord said, a Every plant which My heavenly Father has not planted shall be rooted up" (Matt. 15:13). There are many things that do not have their source in God the Father. When the Lord comes back, they will all be rooted up.
The co-workers, elders, and leading brothers need to have a sober and clear revelation that there is a golden pot in the ark signifying the Fathers divine nature. He is the unique source. We need to know Him as the hidden source of everything. It is very helpful to pray, "Father, I honor You as the unique source. Out from You are all things. Father, You are the unique goal. Unto You are all things." It is quite marvelous when the elders and the saints in the church gather together in vital, praying groups. They come into the Holy of Holies to the ark and touch the pot, praying that God in His nature would be the source of everything they would bring into the church. It is a blessing to be in a church under the administration of such an eldership.
The Hidden Manna Signifying Our Experience of Christ as Our Life Supply
The Hidden Manna Being the Hidden Christ in His Hidden Life Supply
The hidden manna in the golden pot signifies our experience of Christ as our life supply in the deepest way (Heb. 9:4c; Exo. 16:33-34; Rev. 2:17). The hidden manna is the hidden Christ in His hidden life supply, the topmost portion of Christ hidden in the divine nature (Col. 3:3-4). "For you died, and your life is hidden with Christ in God" (v. 3). We cannot have any enduring substance if everything in our Christian life is visible and on the surface. Christ is hidden in God, and we are in Christ. In Christ we are hidden in God, feeding upon a very special portion of the life supply. Our experiences of this eventually produce a white stone with a name on it known only to us (Rev. 2:17). This name will be the interpretation of our secret, hidden experiences of Christ about which we could never preach, talk, or fellowship. Only God and we know the meaning of our deep, hidden history of enjoying the hidden manna. What is hidden in the depths, and what we are in the depths, is for God's hearts desire.
Our Father is in secret and sees in secret (Matt. 6:4, 6, 18). The prayer to the Father in secret will one day be rewarded by Him. We pray prayers no one knows we are praying. We go though things no one knows we are going though. We even lay aside the "dealt-with" look. We appear not to fast. We appear not to be suffering. This becomes a further cross because there is no sympathy in a natural way. Our comfort is the God of resurrection. In all this we eat the hidden manna for the sake of God's economy. The Christian life should be a hidden life, a life hidden with Christ in God.
We should begin to have some minutes alone with the Lord every day. Do not despise such a time, even if you begin with only five minutes, because this will be five minutes of intimate, personal fellowship with Him. If you do not know how to do this, tell Him, "I do not know how to do this. Lord, I do not know how to be hidden. I do not know how to relate to You in a hidden way." He may say, "I know that. I will shepherd you. I am your Master. I will disciple you. I will pray for you and minister to you. I will train you. It is so precious to Me that some would go against today’s world and today's religion with its outward vainglory to be deeply hidden in Me for My heart's desire. I know this is unfamiliar territory for you, but I am comfortable here. I have My being here, and I died to bring you here. I prayed to the Father that where I am you also may be."
Enjoying the Hidden Christ as a Special Portion of Heavenly Food Reserved for His Overcoming Seekers
If we abide in the Holy of Holies, we will enjoy the hidden Christ as a special portion of heavenly food reserved for His overcoming seekers (Rev. 2:17), When we are in the Holy of Holies, we are beyond. the world, the outward situation, our natural being, our self-consciousness, and ourselves. We are hidden with Christ in God, This is one of our bequests.
Being Incorporated into the Universal, Divine-human Incorporation by Eating Christ as the Hidden Manna
By eating Christ as the hidden manna, we are incorporated into the universal, divine-human incorporation (John 14:20). We are able to understand the deeper and higher truths because we have an innate ability to know God. We may temporarily need some outward teaching, but to apprehend these things is not a matter of human brilliance. It is a matter of being a disciple in the divine life with its ability to know God. By this inward knowing, we can understand the three words: union, mingling, and incorporation. Union implies something organic, a matter of life, such as a branch in the vine (15:4), Mingling refers to the divine and human natures mingled together as in the meal offering (Lev. 2:4). Incorporation is an intimate union and mingling in which persons dwell in each other in coinherence. This means that the Father dwells in the Son and the Son dwells in the Father. The Triune God, the Divine Trinity, is an incorporation because the Father, the Son, and. the Spirit mutually indwell one another and work together as one. In the physical realm there is not such an incorporation. Nevertheless, as the issue of Christ's glorification in His resurrection, He, as the embodiment of the processed Triune God, brought the Divine Trinity as a divine incorporation into our spirit. He brought us as a new creation into this divine incorporation, and we began to live a life of coinherence,of abiding in Christ and Christ abiding in us (John 15:5)e This incorporation is not only divine but also human.
The tabernacle in the Old Testament is a sign of the universal incorporation* The more we eat of Christ as the hidden manna, the more we are incorporated into this universal incorporation. The ark is inside the Holy of Holies, and the golden pot is inside the ark. The hidden manna is within the golden pot. The hidden manna is Christ as our life supply, but it is in the golden pot, signifying the Father s nature. Christ is in the Father. But the golden pot, signifying the Father with His nature, is in the ark, which signifies Christ. Therefore Christ is in the Father, and the Father is in Chris. The manna, Christ, is in the golden pot, the Father, which is in the ark, which is Christ.
As we go inward to eat the hidden Christ, we will discover that more of our day is spent abiding in the vine. This happens by the law of life, which operates through our eating of the hidden manna and our experience of the budding rod. By simply enjoying Christ in a hidden way, we are incorporated more and more into the divine incorporation.. Though we may do our job outwardly like anyone else, intrinsically we are different from everyone else because we are in the real incorporation. We thus begin to live a married life,have a family life,and have our free time as part of the divine-human incorporation. This happens by the effortless way of eating Christ as the hidden manna.
The Budding Rod Signifying Our Experience of Christ in His Resurrection as Our Acceptance by God for Authority in the God-given Ministry
The budding rod signifies our experience of Christ in His resurrection as our acceptance by God for authority in the God-given ministry (Heb. 9:4d). It is helpful to read Numbers 17:1-10, which says:
And Jehovah spoke to Moses, saying, Speak to the sons of Israel, and take from them rods, one for each fathers' house, from all their leaders according to their fathers' houses, twelve rods. Write each mans name upon his rod, and write Aarons name upon the rod of Levi. For there shall be one rod for the head of each fathers' house. Then you shall lay them in the tent of meeting before the testimony, where I meet with you. And the rod of the man whom I choose shall bud; thus I will make to cease from Me the murmurings of the sons of Israel, which they murmur against you. And Moses spoke to the sons of Israel; and all their leaders gave him rods, one rod for each leader, according to their fathers' houses, twelve rods; and the rod of Aaron was among their rods. And Moses laid the rods before Jehovah in the tent of the testimony. And on the next day Moses went into the tent of the testimony; and behold, the rod of Aaron for the house of Levi had budded and put forth buds and produced blossoms, and it bore ripe almonds. And Moses brought all the rods out from before Jehovah to all the sons of Israel; and they looked, and each man took his rod. And Jehovah said to Moses, Put back the rod of Aaron before the testimony, to be kept as a sign for the sons of rebellion, that you may make an end of their murmurings against Me, lest they die.
There was a big rebellion in chapter sixteen. As a result, the earth opened and the leaders of the rebellion were taken alive into Sheol (16:29-33), fire from God consumed two hundred and fifty princes (v. 35), and a plague broke out and destroyed fourteen thousand seven hundred people (vv. 46-49). These were negative miracles to show God's judgment on rebellion. After these signs, there was still the need for God's vindication of His leadership in the ministry, which came in an unusual way. The twelve rods were placed before Jehovah in the tent of testimony for a long night. At the beginning they were indistinguishable, except for the names written on them. But in the morning one and only one rod had budded. It sprouted, put forth buds, produced blossoms, and bore ripe almonds. The budding rod is a type of Christ, the resurrected One, who uses His authority to impart life to others. This is a picture of Christ in His resurrection life as the greatest budding rod in the universe. After His resurrection He said, 'All authority has been given to Me in heaven and on earth (Matt. 28:18).
Today there is the need of God's acceptance for the God-given ministry. The New Testament ministry has a God-vindicated standing and authority, which is Christ in resurrection as a sprouting, budding, and blossoming almond-bearing rod. This kind of manifestation of resurrection life is God's sign to His people concerning where His ministry is today.
A horrible exercise of authority exists today in the world. It is not with a budding rod but with a dead stick, not for "feeding" but for "beating." It is dreadful and despicable when ones taking the lead in the church or in the ministry use a "rod" to "beat" the other saints. This is most serious. Such a one the Lord may "cut asunder and appoint his portion with the hypocrites" (Matt. 24:51). There is another kind of authority and leadership that is built up by the resurrection life of Christ, and the rod is used for feeding, not beating. Brother Witness Lee was such an example of this kind of authority and leadership among us. In Christ he was a budding rod, and he always used the rod to feed us. He never controlled anyone or any church. His ministry was a ministry of feeding.
In Numbers 17 we see that the rod of Aaron was for the house of Levi (v. 8), indicating something corporate. A corporate entity has been chosen by God to carry out His ministry in a corporate way. It was Aaron's rod that budded because he was taking the lead in the priesthood, but that budding rod was for the house of Levi. We see the fulfillment of this with the New Testament apostles. Acts 1 speaks twice of this ministry (vv. 17, 25). The apostles had a corporate ministry. Because Judas turned aside, another had to be chosen to fill up what w a s lacking in this ministry. The one chosen had to be a follower from the very beginning and a witness with the eleven of the resurrection of Christ (w. 21-22). In this portion of Acts we see the apostles, their ministry, and the resurrection of Christ mentioned together. .
In 2 Corinthians 4 Paul says, "Therefore having this ministry as we have been shown mercy, we do not lose heart" (v. 1). He did riot say, "I have the budding rod. I am the leader. I am the authority." Paul went on to speak about death operating in the apostles, but life in the believers (v. 12), Paul and his co-workers went through death that life could be ministered into the church. In chapter one of 2 Corinthians he spoke of the God of resurrection (v. 9). We therefore have in Paul's ministry God's vindication and acceptance through Christ and with Christ as the God-given authority for the God-given ministry.
The Budding, Blossoming, Fruit-yielding Rod Signifying the Resurrection Life of Christ with Its Authority
The budding, blossoming, fruit-yielding rod signifies the resurrection life of Christ with its authority (Num. 17:8; John 11:25), In a letter dated March. 24, 1997, entitled "A Letter of Fellowship with Thanks," Brother Lee wrote the following: "The Lord has shown me that He has prepared many brothers who will serve as fellow slaves with me in a blended way. I feel that this is the Lord's sovereign provision for His Body, and the up-to-date way to fulfill His ministry." He signed the letter, "Your fellow partaker in experiencing and gaining Christ." Brother Lee was burdened for a group of blended co-workers. He did not give an exact list or a finite number. Our Brother Lee is gone to be with the Lord, but we all must depend upon the grace of God and continue to serve as fellow slaves in the new covenant ministry in a blended way.
In the Old Testament there was a corporate ministry with a budding rod. In the New Testament there is the apostles' ministry, a corporate ministry. Eventually, this ministry is for all the members of the Body of Christ.
The Budding Rod Being the Issue of the Enjoyment of the Hidden Manna
The budding rod is the issue of the enjoyment of the hidden manna; thus, the more we enjoy the hidden Christ, the more we will experience the budding, blossoming, and fruit-bearing of the resurrected. Christ. We need to experience Christ as the budding rod so that we would. have the resurrection life with which to feed God's people. We should never beat the believers or discipline them; rather, we should use the God…given authority to give them "almonds" to give them life and. the life supply. If the saints are dead, then we should die for them as Paul did, "Death operates in us, but life in you" (2 Cor. 4:12). This is the new covenant ministry.
The Tablets of the Covenant Signifying Our Experience of Christ as the Inner Law of Life
The tablets of the covenant signify our experience of Christ as the inner law of life, which infuses the divine nature and attributes into our being, conforms us to the image of the firstborn Son of God,and makes us the corporate reproduction of Christ, the standard model (Heb. 9:4e; 8:10; Rom. 8:2, 29). Christ has passed through a process to enter into His perfection and glorification, and now, as the law of life, He is repeating this process within us, bringing us into His perfection and glorification (Heb, 2:10). As the law of life works Christ into every part of our being, causing Christ to be formed in us, God is wrought into man, man is mingled with God, and. God and man become one entity-this is.the most secret mystery in the whole universe (Gal 4:19; John 14:20; 17:21 -24). In the Holy of Holies we enjoy the hidden manna, the hidden manna produces the budding rod, and the budding rod. with resurrection life brings us into the real experience of the law of life, which makes us more than conquerors. Thus, we are "sonized" to become the corporate expression of Christ, the firstborn Son of God.
PARTICIPATING IN THE INTERCEDING LIFE OF CHRIST AS THE CENTER OF THE DIVINE ADMINISTRATION SIGNIFIED BY THE GOLDEN INCENSE ALTAR
At the golden incense altar, we participate in the interceding life of Christ as the center of the divine administration (Heb, 9:4a; Exo. 30:1-10). Revelation 8 says, "And another Angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer, and much incense was given to Him to offer with the prayers of all the saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne. And the smoke of the incense went up with the prayers of the saints out of the hand of the Angel before God. And the Angel took the censer and filled it with the fire of the altar and cast it to the earth; and there were thunders and voices and lightnings and an earthquake." (vv. 3-5), This is Christ as another Angel in God's economy, sent by God to carry out His administration on the earth. Christ does so by ministering to God as the High Priest with the prayers of His saints. Christ ministers at the incense altar that is before the throne (v. 3), showing that the incense altar is for God's administration. He receives the bowls that are the prayers of the saints, adds to them Himself as the incense for God's acceptance, and presents these to God who is on the administrating throne. Immediately, there is a reaction affecting the situation on earth. This is Christ in His interceding life as the center of the divine administration.
The Incense Altar, Which Is Related to Prayer, Belonging to the Holy of Holies
The writer of Hebrews regarded the incense altar, which is related to prayer, as belonging to the Holy of Holies (9:3-4, see note 1 on verse 4 of the Recovery Version; 1 Kings 6:22; Luke 1:10-11), In Hebrews 9 we are not told that the incense altar was in the Holy of Holies, but that the Holy of Holies had the incense altar. This means that although the position of the incense altar was in the Holy Place, its function was for the Holy of Holies. In this section of Hebrews, the incense altar is prominent because in the experience of Christ signified by the incense altar we cooperate with Christ's intercession to carry out God's administration.
The Incense Altar Signifying Christ as the Intercessor to Maintain the Relationship between God and His People
The incense altar signifies Christ as the Intercessor to maintain the relationship between God and His people (Rom. 8:34; Heb. 7:25). The incense altar is a type of the person of Christ. It signifies Christ praying, Christ interceding (John 17:1-26). The Gospel of John shows us the fulfillment of the tabernacle and the offerings. The showbread table, which signifies Christ as the bread of life, is revealed in John 6:35. The lampstand, signifying Christ as the light of life, is indicated in John 8:12. The laver, which signifies the washing of the Holy Spirit, is in John 13 with the washing of the disciples5 feet (v. 5). The Holy of Holies is revealed in John 14一16. Christ's intercession, revealed in His prayer in John 17, is the reality of the incense altar.
The incense altar ushers us into the Holy of Holies to experience Christ as the ark. We then go on to experience Christ as the manna, the budding rod, and the law of life. This makes us deeply one with God for the carrying out of His economy. We then realize that there is an operation going on within our spirit. This operation is the anointing, the moving of the interceding Christ with whom we are one. He desires that we be one with Him as the Intercessor to pray for whatever is on the heart of God. It is not for us to initiate. It is not for us to offer any kind of natural prayer. We are one with Him.
In John 15 the Lord said, a If you abide in Me and My words abide in you, ask whatever you will, and it shall be done for you" (v. 7). This can be so because we are one with Christ. What is on God's heart is in our heart. What He wants is what we want. What He is burdened for is what we are burdened for. The Lord wants us to join with Him in His ministry of intercession, so He makes us one with Him; then He infuses His feeling into us and mingles it with our own being; ultimately, we pray it out. This becomes our participation in Christ's intercession.
Enjoying Christ as the Incense Altar
The Incense Altar Being the Place from Which the Activities in All Other Places in the Tabernacle and the Outer Court Are Motivated
We need to enjoy Christ not only as the table, the lampstand, and the ark but also as the incense altar. As a divine motor, the incense altar is the place from which the activities in all other places in the tabernacle and the outer court are motivated. How do the altar and the laver in the outer court work in our experience? They work in our experience when we pray. They work in others when we pray for them. If we are burdened that someone would be saved and come to the bronze altar, we must go to the incense altar and pray. If a certain person needs light and they cannot respond to any human word, we should go to the incense altar and pray. If we are burdened that a number of saints have remained so long in their natural life, untouched and unbroken, not having learned the lesson of the rending of the veil, then we must go to the incense altar and intercede for them.
Our talk and criticism about the saints will not minister life to anyone. The only thing that will bring in a river of grace is prayer at the incense altar. We do not need others' examination or criticism; we need prayers. Our prayers are the motor that makes everything work in our lives and in the lives of those for whom we are burdened. How marvelous to come together in vital groups and motivate things to happen in the church and in the lives of so many people! This is a divine reality, a bequest, and also the responsibility of the church.
Not Having the Incense Altar, Making All of the Aspects of the Tabernacle and the Outer Court Ineffective in Our Experience
If we do not have the incense altar, none of the aspects of the tabernacle and the outer court will be effective in our experience. Our prayers will motivate these things for our experience in the midst of our often concrete, humdrum, and ordinary existence. If our personal prayer cannot do it, we should admit that we cannot get through and call up a vital companion or someone who is willing to be a vital companion. Let us pray together for ourselves, for the saints, for the church, for the churches, and for the whole recovery. We need not only a year of prayer but also a life of prayer, a life of living at the incense altar.
In 1974 Brother Lee went through an agonizing, painful, and costly experience. He testified that as he was praying, the Lord told him to "pray to be prayed for." This is an example of a brother, a faithful slave, living in the Body of Christ, praying that someone else would pray for him. Surely at that time .there were some hidden intercessors who picked up the intuitive sense and prayed for him. Similarly, during the months when our Brother Lee was passing away, the Lord brought the whole recovery to the incense altar.
Christ's Interceding Life, His Prayer Life, Being the Center of the Divine Administration Christ's interceding life, His prayer life, is the center of the divine administration (Rev, 8:3). The incense altar is the center of God's operation in the universe. The prayer life of Christ is the center of God's execution of His government. In Exodus 17, Amalek attacked Israel. "And Moses said unto Joshua, Choose us out men, and go out, fight with Amalek: tomorrow I will stand on the top of the hill with the rod of God in mine hand" (v. 9). Moses used the rod of God not to beat others but to pray prayers of authority. However, he became tired and his hands fell. When his hands fell, Amalek prevailed; when his hands were uplifted, Joshua prevailed. Aaron and Hur then formed a "vital group." They sat Moses down on a rock and helped him keep his hands up. Are we too proud to admit that we need the saints to hold up our hands? We should also have the grace and the humility to hold up someone elses hands. What a blessed, vital coordination!
While Daniel was in Babylon, he read in the prophecy of Jeremiah that after seventy years there would be a return from captivity (Dan. 9:2). Daniels response was not to sit around, watch, and wait to see what would happen. It was written in the Bible, but it needed to be executed. It was a bequest in the will, but who would join Christ to execute it? Daniel prayed and confessed until he received a. response (w. 20-22), In another case, Daniel prayed for a long time; he prayed three weeks of combative prayer before the answer came (10:1-2,12-13). These illustrations show us how prayer executes God's governmental administration.
The Executing Center of God's Administration Being the Incense Altar, Not the Ark
The executing center of God's administration is the incense altar, not the ark. At the incense altar we care for God and His economy only; our prayer at the incense altar is not about our health, spirituality, marriage, or children— though we .dearly care for these things. When we are at the incense altar, the only thing on our heart is God and His economy. At the incense altar, we pray whatever is transmitted into us by the heavenly Intercessor.
The Prayer Life of Christ Being the Center— the Heavenly White House— of God's Execution of His Government on Earth
The prayer life of Christ is the center— the heavenly White House— of God's execution of His government on earth. We do not care to sit in the Oval Office, but we care to sit with Christ on His throne to intercede with Him.
The Prayer Offered at the Incense Altar Governing the Whole Universe
The prayer offered at the incense altar governs the whole universe, Christ is governing the universe by praying. He knows what is in God's heart. He requests something and the Father answers immediately. If the Lord wants a ruler of Satan to be put down for His move,that ruler will be put down. If He wants to change the world situation, it will be done. If the Lord wants Russia to be opened, it must be opened. The saints from Russia are the issue of Christ's intercession, Christ is governing the universe, but He does not want to do it by Himself Now we are intercessors with Christ. He would like all of the heirs, the partners, the members of His Body, to cooperate with Him in His intercession.
The Corporate Christ, the Body-Christ, the Head. with the Body, Interceding to Carry Out His Intercession
After His resurrection and ascension, the individual Christ became the corporate Christ; thus, before God today not only is the individual Christ interceding, but the corporate Christ, the Body-Christ, the Head with the Body is also interceding to carry out His intercession (1 Cor. 12:12; Acts 12:5312; Rom. 8:26-27). Christ is interceding in the heavens, and the church, which is His Body, is interceding on earth. The intercessor is not only Christ Himself but also Christ with His Body. Consider how much could be accomplished if Christ's intercession were carried out through the corporate Christ joining Him in His intercession, praying according to God in the interceding Spirit.
Praying for the Executing of God's Purpose, for the Carrying Out of the Divine Administration, and for the Dispensing of God's Supplying Grace
If we have this view of the incense altar, our prayer life will be revolutionized, and we will pray for the executing of God's purpose, for the carrying out of the divine administration, and for the dispensing of God's supplying grace. May the elders take the lead to pray in this way, and may the vital groups be ushered into such prayer, According to Matthew 18:18 such prayers will not be begging prayers but binding and loosing prayers. What heaven has bound, we also must bind. What heaven has loosed, we also must loose.
The Prayer of the Corporate Christ Fulfilling God's Purpose, Satisfying His Hunger and Delighting His Heart
The prayer of the corporate Christ fulfills God's purpose, satisfies His hunger, and delights His heart. We want to have a part in fulfilling God's purpose, satisfying His hunger, and delighting His heart. May we all as the Body join Christ the Head in governing the universe through intercessory prayer.
Prayer: Lord, work this word out for Your hearts desire. Raise up all over Your recovery vital groups of intercessors and vital personal intercessors, hidden with Christ in God. Lord, do this to shame and shock Your enemy. Do it as an indictment of today s Christianity. Do it to cause the grumbling of the rebellious ones to cease. Above all, do it for Your own good pleasure, for Your own joy, delight, and happiness. O Father, we pray for Your economy as the corporate Christ, Give us a life of prayer. Recover the intercession of the corporate Christ. We ask this in Your name. Amen.— R. K.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|Outline|对照-目录
　
　
第十九篇　基督是旧约一切祭物的了结、应验和顶替，也是这一切祭物新约的实际
晨兴-大纲|Outline|对照-听抄-目录
读经：来十5～10；约一14，29，四23～24，十四6下
壹　神的旨意是要基督作旧约中一切祭物的顶替，使我们享受祂作一切，而过身体的生活，并实行身体的生活，以建造基督的身体作三一神的生机体─弗一5,9～11,来十7～10,罗十二2：
一　基督顶替旧约一切的祭物，除去旧约一切的预表，立定祂自己作我们的一切，乃是神极大的旨意：
１　旧约在以赛亚五十三章曾预言说，基督要来成为赎罪祭，就是说，要来顶替并了结利未祭司体系的祭物─6，11～12节。
２　神为基督预备了身体，好使基督能将祂自己献给神，以顶替一切的祭物─来十5。
３　基督除去“第一的”，就是旧约的祭牲，为要立定“第二的”，就是新约的祭物─9节：
a　作为“第二的”，基督乃是一切─9节。
b　我们凭这旨意，借基督一次永远献上的身体，得着圣别，使我们得以享受并有分于祂作我们的一切─10节。
二　天神的旨意乃是要我们享受基督，使我们借着祂生机的拯救，得以成为祂团体的复制品─林前一9，贴前五16～18，罗五10，八6。
贰　我们必须享受基督作帐幕，就是可以进入的神，并享受祂作旧约一切祭物的实际，使祂成为我们的真实、真诚，好叫我们以神所要的敬拜来敬拜祂─约一14，四23～24，十四17上：
一　基督作帐幕与一切祭物的应验，乃是宇宙的实际与内容：
１　神成肉体的基督，来作神具体的化身，犹如帐幕，给人接触并进入，而得享神里面的丰富。
２　基督作神的具体化身，将神带给人，使神成为可接触、可摸着、可接受、可经历、可进入、并可享受的。
３　成为肉体来作活帐幕的基督，也是神的羔羊，就是一切祭物的集大成，总和─约一14，29。
４　作为帐幕，基督是可进入的神，也就是神与人相互的住处；作为祭物，基督是我们进入神里面的道路─十四6上。
５　基督是帐幕，将神带给人；基督是祭物，将人带给神，使人可以与神联结、调和并合并。
６　帐幕使我们可以经历神，进入神，与神联结；祭物使我们可以享受神，与神调和。
７　祭物乃是鉴赏基督的人所献给神的礼物，为着他们与神之间有亲密的交通─利一2。
二　我们必须天天操练取用并应用基督作我们的赎罪祭和赎愆祭，这把我们引进对基督作燔祭和素祭的享受里，终极完成于我们对祂作平安祭以及摇祭、举祭和奠祭的享受：
１　赎罪祭表征基督替我们成为罪，在十字架上借着死定罪了罪─利四3，林后五21，罗八3，约一29，三24。
２　赎愆祭表征基督在祂的身体里亲自担当了我们的罪，替我们在十字架上受了神的审判，解决了我们行为上的罪，使我们在罪行上得着赦免─利五6，彼前二24，三18，赛五三5～6，10～11，约四15～18。
３　那完全为着神满足的燔祭，表征基督是神的喜悦和满足，祂在地上的生活绝对为着神─利一3，民二八2～3，约七16～18。
４　素祭表征在人性里的基督并表征祂的生活为人都是正确适度，柔细均匀，清洁无罪的─利二1，4，约七46，十八38，十九4，6。
５　平安祭表征基督是成就和平者，祂为我们受死流血，作了我们与神之间的和平与交通，使我们能与神共同享受基督，在祂里面与神相安、相交，神人同得满足─利三1，弗二14～15，约十二1～3，二十21，启二一2。
６　摇祭表征在爱中复活的基督─利七30，十15。
７　举祭表征在升天与高举里有能力的基督─利七32，出二九27，弗一21。
８　奠祭表征基督成为献祭者的享受，使献祭的人被基督这属天的酒所充满，甚至使他成为献给神的酒，作神的享受和满足─出二九40，民二八7～10，赛五三12，腓二17，提后四6，士九13。
叁　我们必须过一种合乎神的心和旨意的生活，天天享受基督作一切祭物的实际，为着达到三一神的神圣目标，就是把我们众人都带到祂里面，使我们以祂作我们的居所，并让祂以我们作祂的居所，为着祂宇宙神人二性扩大的合并─约十四23，启二一3，22。

TOP-晨兴-大纲|Outline|对照-听抄-目录
　
　
GENERAL SUBJECT
The Crystallization-Study of Epistle to the Hebrews
Message Nineteen
Christ as the Termination, Fulfillment, Replacement, and New Testament Reality of All the Old Testament Offerings
晨兴-纲目|对照-听抄-目录
Scripture Reading: Heb. 10:5-10; John 1:14, 29; 4:23-24; 14:6a
I.　The will of God is to have Christ as the replacement for all the offerings in the Old Testament so that we may enjoy Him as everything in living and practicing the Body life for the building up of the Body of Christ as the organism of the Triune God—Eph. 1:5, 9-11; Heb. 10:7-10; Rom. 12:2:
A.　Christ's replacing of all the Old Testament offerings, taking away all the Old Testament types and establishing Himself as everything to us, is God's great will:
1.　The Old Testament predicted in Isaiah 53 that Christ would come to be the sacrifice for sin, that is, to replace and terminate the Levitical sacrifices—vv. 6, 11-12.
2.　God prepared a body for Christ so that He could offer Himself to God to replace all the offerings—Heb. 10:5.
3.　Christ took away "the first," the sacrifices of the old covenant, that He might establish Himself as "the second," the sacrifice of the new covenant—v. 9:
a.　As "the second" Christ is everything—v. 9.
b.　By this will we have been sanctified through the offering of Christ's body once for all so that we may enjoy and partake of Him as our everything—v. 10.
B.　The will of God today is simply for us to enjoy Christ so that we may become the corporate reproduction of Himself through His organic salvation—1 Cor. 1:9; 1 Thes. 5:16-18; Rom. 5:10; 8:6.
II.　We need to enjoy Christ as the tabernacle, the enterable God, and as the reality of all the offerings of the Old Testament so that He may become our genuineness and sincerity for us to worship God with the worship that He seeks—John 1:14; 4:23-24; 14:17a:
A.　Christ as the fulfillment of the tabernacle and all the offerings is the reality and content of the universe:
1.　Christ, the incarnate God, came as the embodiment of God, as illustrated by the tabernacle, so that man could contact Him and enter into Him to enjoy the riches contained in God.
2.　Christ as the embodiment of God brought God to man to make God contactable, touchable, receivable, experienceable, enterable, and enjoyable.
3.　Christ who was incarnated to be the living tabernacle is also the Lamb of God, the aggregate, the totality, of all the offerings— John 1:14, 29.
4.　As the tabernacle Christ is the enterable God, the mutual abode of God and man, and as the offerings He is the way for us to enter into God—14:6a.
5.　Christ as the tabernacle brings God to man, and Christ as the offerings brings man to God so that man may be united, mingled, and incorporated with God.
6.　The tabernacle is good for us to experience, enter into, and join to God, and the offerings are good for us to enjoy God and mingle with God.
7.　The offerings are presents to God by the appreciators of Christ for their intimate fellowship with Him—Lev. 1:2.
B.　We daily need to practice taking and applying Christ as our sin offering and trespass offering, which will usher us into the enjoyment of Christ as the burnt offering and the meal offering, consummating in our enjoyment of Him as the peace offering plus the wave offering, heave offering, and drink offering:
1.　The sin offering signifies that Christ was made sin for us that through His death on the cross, sin might be condemned—Lev. 4:3; 2 Cor. 5:21; Rom. 8:3; John 1:29; 3:14.
2.　The trespass offering signifies that Christ bore our sins in His own body and was judged by God on the cross to deal with our sinful deeds that we might be forgiven in our sinful conduct—Lev. 5:6; 1 Pet. 2:24; 3:18; Isa. 53:5-6, 10-11; John 4:15-18.
3.　The burnt offering, which was wholly for God's satisfaction, signifies Christ as God's pleasure and satisfaction, as the One whose living on earth was absolutely for God—Lev. 1:3; Num. 28:2-3; John 7:16-18.
4.　The meal offering signifies Christ in His humanity and in His human living, which was proper, even, tender, fine, balanced, pure, and sinless—Lev. 2:1, 4; John 7:46; 18:38; 19:4, 6.
5.　The peace offering signifies Christ as the Peacemaker, the One who became the peace and the fellowship between God and us by shedding His blood and dying for us, enabling us to enjoy Christ with God and to have fellowship with God in Christ for our mutual satisfaction with God—Lev. 3:1; Eph. 2:14-15; John 12:1-3; 20:21; Rev. 21:2.
6.　The wave offering signifies the resurrected Christ in love—Lev. 7:30; 10:15.
7.　The heave offering signifies the powerful Christ in ascension and exaltation—Lev. 7:32; Exo. 29:27; Eph. 1:21.
8.　The drink offering signifies Christ as the enjoyment of the offerer, enabling the offerer to be filled with Christ as the heavenly wine and even to become the wine offered to God for His enjoyment and satisfaction—Exo. 29:40; Num. 28:7-10; Isa. 53:12; Phil. 2:17; 2 Tim. 4:6; Judg. 9:13.
III.　We need to live a life according to God's heart and will by daily enjoying Christ as the reality of all the offerings for the divine goal of the Triune God, which is to bring us all into Himself that we may take Him as our dwelling place and allow Him to take us as His dwelling place for His universal, enlarged, divine-human incorporation—John 14:23; Rev. 21:3, 22.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|对照-听抄-目录
　
　
第十九篇　基督是旧约一切祭物的了结、应验和顶替，也是这一切祭物新约的实际
Christ as the Termination, Fulfillment, Replacement, and New Testament Reality of All the Old Testament Offerings
晨兴-纲目|outline-听抄-目录
读经：来十5～10；约一14，29，四23～24，十四6下
壹　神的旨意是要基督作旧约中一切祭物的顶替，使我们享受祂作一切，而过身体的生活，并实行身体的生活，以建造基督的身体作三一神的生机体─弗一5,9～11,来十7～10,罗十二2：
I.　The will of God is to have Christ as the replacement for all the offerings in the Old Testament so that we may enjoy Him as everything in living and practicing the Body life for the building up of the Body of Christ as the organism of the Triune God—Eph. 1:5, 9-11; Heb. 10:7-10; Rom. 12:2:
一　基督顶替旧约一切的祭物，除去旧约一切的预表，立定祂自己作我们的一切，乃是神极大的旨意：
A.　Christ's replacing of all the Old Testament offerings, taking away all the Old Testament types and establishing Himself as everything to us, is God's great will:
１　旧约在以赛亚五十三章曾预言说，基督要来成为赎罪祭，就是说，要来顶替并了结利未祭司体系的祭物─6，11～12节。
1.　The Old Testament predicted in Isaiah 53 that Christ would come to be the sacrifice for sin, that is, to replace and terminate the Levitical sacrifices—vv. 6, 11-12.
２　神为基督预备了身体，好使基督能将祂自己献给神，以顶替一切的祭物─来十5。
2.　God prepared a body for Christ so that He could offer Himself to God to replace all the offerings—Heb. 10:5.
３　基督除去“第一的”，就是旧约的祭牲，为要立定“第二的”，就是新约的祭物─9节：
3.　Christ took away "the first," the sacrifices of the old covenant, that He might establish Himself as "the second," the sacrifice of the new covenant—v. 9:
a　作为“第二的”，基督乃是一切─9节。
a.　As "the second" Christ is everything—v. 9.
b　我们凭这旨意，借基督一次永远献上的身体，得着圣别，使我们得以享受并有分于祂作我们的一切─10节。
b.　By this will we have been sanctified through the offering of Christ's body once for all so that we may enjoy and partake of Him as our everything—v. 10.
二　天神的旨意乃是要我们享受基督，使我们借着祂生机的拯救，得以成为祂团体的复制品─林前一9，贴前五16～18，罗五10，八6。
B.　The will of God today is simply for us to enjoy Christ so that we may become the corporate reproduction of Himself through His organic salvation—1 Cor. 1:9; 1 Thes. 5:16-18; Rom. 5:10; 8:6.
贰　我们必须享受基督作帐幕，就是可以进入的神，并享受祂作旧约一切祭物的实际，使祂成为我们的真实、真诚，好叫我们以神所要的敬拜来敬拜祂─约一14，四23～24，十四17上：
II.　We need to enjoy Christ as the tabernacle, the enterable God, and as the reality of all the offerings of the Old Testament so that He may become our genuineness and sincerity for us to worship God with the worship that He seeks—John 1:14; 4:23-24; 14:17a:
一　基督作帐幕与一切祭物的应验，乃是宇宙的实际与内容：
A.　Christ as the fulfillment of the tabernacle and all the offerings is the reality and content of the universe:
１　神成肉体的基督，来作神具体的化身，犹如帐幕，给人接触并进入，而得享神里面的丰富。
1.　Christ, the incarnate God, came as the embodiment of God, as illustrated by the tabernacle, so that man could contact Him and enter into Him to enjoy the riches contained in God.
２　基督作神的具体化身，将神带给人，使神成为可接触、可摸着、可接受、可经历、可进入、并可享受的。
2.　Christ as the embodiment of God brought God to man to make God contactable, touchable, receivable, experienceable, enterable, and enjoyable.
３　成为肉体来作活帐幕的基督，也是神的羔羊，就是一切祭物的集大成，总和─约一14，29。
3.　Christ who was incarnated to be the living tabernacle is also the Lamb of God, the aggregate, the totality, of all the offerings— John 1:14, 29.
４　作为帐幕，基督是可进入的神，也就是神与人相互的住处；作为祭物，基督是我们进入神里面的道路─十四6上。
4.　As the tabernacle Christ is the enterable God, the mutual abode of God and man, and as the offerings He is the way for us to enter into God—14:6a.
５　基督是帐幕，将神带给人；基督是祭物，将人带给神，使人可以与神联结、调和并合并。
5.　Christ as the tabernacle brings God to man, and Christ as the offerings brings man to God so that man may be united, mingled, and incorporated with God.
６　帐幕使我们可以经历神，进入神，与神联结；祭物使我们可以享受神，与神调和。
6.　The tabernacle is good for us to experience, enter into, and join to God, and the offerings are good for us to enjoy God and mingle with God.
７　祭物乃是鉴赏基督的人所献给神的礼物，为着他们与神之间有亲密的交通─利一2。
7.　The offerings are presents to God by the appreciators of Christ for their intimate fellowship with Him—Lev. 1:2.
二　我们必须天天操练取用并应用基督作我们的赎罪祭和赎愆祭，这把我们引进对基督作燔祭和素祭的享受里，终极完成于我们对祂作平安祭以及摇祭、举祭和奠祭的享受：
B.　We daily need to practice taking and applying Christ as our sin offering and trespass offering, which will usher us into the enjoyment of Christ as the burnt offering and the meal offering, consummating in our enjoyment of Him as the peace offering plus the wave offering, heave offering, and drink offering:
１　赎罪祭表征基督替我们成为罪，在十字架上借着死定罪了罪─利四3，林后五21，罗八3，约一29，三24。
1.　The sin offering signifies that Christ was made sin for us that through His death on the cross, sin might be condemned—Lev. 4:3; 2 Cor. 5:21; Rom. 8:3; John 1:29; 3:14.
２　赎愆祭表征基督在祂的身体里亲自担当了我们的罪，替我们在十字架上受了神的审判，解决了我们行为上的罪，使我们在罪行上得着赦免─利五6，彼前二24，三18，赛五三5～6，10～11，约四15～18。
2.　The trespass offering signifies that Christ bore our sins in His own body and was judged by God on the cross to deal with our sinful deeds that we might be forgiven in our sinful conduct—Lev. 5:6; 1 Pet. 2:24; 3:18; Isa. 53:5-6, 10-11; John 4:15-18.
３　那完全为着神满足的燔祭，表征基督是神的喜悦和满足，祂在地上的生活绝对为着神─利一3，民二八2～3，约七16～18。
3.　The burnt offering, which was wholly for God's satisfaction, signifies Christ as God's pleasure and satisfaction, as the One whose living on earth was absolutely for God—Lev. 1:3; Num. 28:2-3; John 7:16-18.
４　素祭表征在人性里的基督并表征祂的生活为人都是正确适度，柔细均匀，清洁无罪的─利二1，4，约七46，十八38，十九4，6。
4.　The meal offering signifies Christ in His humanity and in His human living, which was proper, even, tender, fine, balanced, pure, and sinless—Lev. 2:1, 4; John 7:46; 18:38; 19:4, 6.
５　平安祭表征基督是成就和平者，祂为我们受死流血，作了我们与神之间的和平与交通，使我们能与神共同享受基督，在祂里面与神相安、相交，神人同得满足─利三1，弗二14～15，约十二1～3，二十21，启二一2。
5.　The peace offering signifies Christ as the Peacemaker, the One who became the peace and the fellowship between God and us by shedding His blood and dying for us, enabling us to enjoy Christ with God and to have fellowship with God in Christ for our mutual satisfaction with God—Lev. 3:1; Eph. 2:14-15; John 12:1-3; 20:21; Rev. 21:2.
６　摇祭表征在爱中复活的基督─利七30，十15。
6.　The wave offering signifies the resurrected Christ in love—Lev. 7:30; 10:15.
７　举祭表征在升天与高举里有能力的基督─利七32，出二九27，弗一21。
7.　The heave offering signifies the powerful Christ in ascension and exaltation—Lev. 7:32; Exo. 29:27; Eph. 1:21.
８　奠祭表征基督成为献祭者的享受，使献祭的人被基督这属天的酒所充满，甚至使他成为献给神的酒，作神的享受和满足─出二九40，民二八7～10，赛五三12，腓二17，提后四6，士九13。
8.　The drink offering signifies Christ as the enjoyment of the offerer, enabling the offerer to be filled with Christ as the heavenly wine and even to become the wine offered to God for His enjoyment and satisfaction—Exo. 29:40; Num. 28:7-10; Isa. 53:12; Phil. 2:17; 2 Tim. 4:6; Judg. 9:13.
叁　我们必须过一种合乎神的心和旨意的生活，天天享受基督作一切祭物的实际，为着达到三一神的神圣目标，就是把我们众人都带到祂里面，使我们以祂作我们的居所，并让祂以我们作祂的居所，为着祂宇宙神人二性扩大的合并─约十四23，启二一3，22。
III.　We need to live a life according to God's heart and will by daily enjoying Christ as the reality of all the offerings for the divine goal of the Triune God, which is to bring us all into Himself that we may take Him as our dwelling place and allow Him to take us as His dwelling place for His universal, enlarged, divine-human incorporation—John 14:23; Rev. 21:3, 22.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|outline-听抄-目录
　
　
MESSAGE NINETEEN
CHRIST AS THE TERMINATION, FULFILLMENT, REPLACEMENT, AND NEW TESTAMENT REALITY OF ALL THE OLD TESTAMENT OFFERINGS
晨兴-纲目|Outline|对照-目录
文档OCR所得，仅供追求参考
Prayer: Lord, we love You so much. Thank You for the blood of the covenant. Lord, apply the blood on our behalf. We trust in You to usher us into the Holy of Holies. We want to spend this time gazing on You. Grant us a spirit of wisdom and revelation to see more of Your glorious person. Lord, we pray for one another. We pray that You would strengthen us all into our inner man, give life to our mortal bodies, and grant us an ear to hear what You are speaking to the churches. Amen.
THE WILL OF GOD BEING TO HAVE CHRIST AS THE REPLACEMENT FOR ALL THE OFFERINGS, SO THAT WE MAY ENJOY HIM AS EVERYTHING IN LIVING AND PRACTICING THE BODY LIFE FOR THE BUILDING UP OF THE BODY OF CHRIST AS THE ORGANISM OF THE TRIUNE GOD
The will of God is to have Christ as the replacement for all the offerings in the Old Testament so that we may enjoy Him as everything in living and practicing the Body life for the building up of the Body of Christ as the organism of the Triune God (Eph. 1:5, 9-11; Heb. 10:7-10; Rom. 12:2). The will of God is His eternal, determined intention. Most believers consider and want to know what God's will is for their own lives. But few recognize that God has an eternal, determined intention that is much greater than where we live, what home we buy, or which person we marry. These are important matters, and we should pray about them and follow the Lord according to the sense of life. But in order to know God's eternal, determined intention, we need a revelation. Such a revelation will change our lives and cause us to be a people who live our lives according to His hearts desire. Three portions of the Word show us God's will in a particular way. The book of Ephesians speaks of the "good pleasure of His will" (1:5), the "mystery of His will" (v. 9), and the "counsel of His will" (v. 11). God's will in Ephesians is to have a Body for Christ (w. 22-23). The Body of Christ is for His enlargement, expansion, expression, reproduction, and duplication. Since God's will is to have the reality of the Body of Christ, we need to consecrate ourselves to live in the Body. Hebrews 10 also shows us the will of God. Verse 7 says, "Then I said, Behold, I have come (in the roll of the book it is written concerning Me) to do Your will, O God." Christ is the only One who is absolutely for God. God's will in the book of Hebrews is for Christ to replace all the sacrifices and offerings and all the types of the Old Testament. Christ came as the reality of the offerings and types so that we can enjoy Him as everything to us. Simply speaking, God's will is for us to enjoy Christ. We can also see the will of God in Romans 12:2, which says, "And do not be fashioned according to this age, but be transformed by the renewing of the mind that you may prove what the will of God is, that which is good and well pleasing and perfect." The will of God in Romans 12 is that we live and practice the Body life in the church life. If we will assimilate these three portions of the Word into our being, we will have a dear view of God's will.
God's Great Will Being Christ's Replacing of All the Old Testament Offerings, Taking Away All the Old Testament Types and Establishing Himself as Everything to Us
Christ Coming to Be the Sacrifice for Sin, Replacing and Terminating the Levitical Sacrifices
Christs replacing of all the Old Testament offerings, taking away all the Old Testament types and establishing Himself as everything to us, is God's great will. The Old Testament predicted in Isaiah 53 that Christ would come to be the sacrifice for sin, that is, to replace and terminate the Levitical sacrifices (vv. 6, 11-12), Approximately seven hundred years before the birth of Christ, the book of Isaiah prophesied in detail concerning many aspects of Christ; therefore, many expositors refer to Isaiah as "the fifth Gospel."
God Preparing a Body for Christ So That He Could Offer Himself to God to Replace All the Offerings, and Christ Taking Away "the First," the Sacrifices of the Old Covenant, That He Might Establish Him self as "the Second," the Sacrifice of the New Covenant
God prepared a body for Christ so that He could offer Himself to God to replace all the offerings (Heb. 10:5). Christ took away "the first," the sacrifices of the old covenant, that He might establish Himself as "the second,n the sacrifice of the new covenant (v. 9). As "the second,n Christ is everything. By this will we have been sanctified through the offering of Christ's body once for all so that we may enjoy and partake of Him as our everything (v. 10).
The Will of God Being for Us to Enjoy Christ That We May Become the Corporate Reproduction of Himself through His Organic Salvation
The will of God today is simply for us to enjoy Christ so that we may become the corporate reproduction of Himself through His organic salvation (1 Thes. 5:16-18; Rom. 5:10; 8:6). In 1 Corinthians 1:9, Paul tells us that we were called into the fellowship of His Son, Jesus Christ our Lord. The word fellowship means joint participation, common participation. We participate in Christ and we enjoy Christ together. The situation of the church in Corinth was a mess. There were many problems among the saints. If we would have written the book of 1 Corinthians, we might have tried to correct the saints by telling them to "shape up!" However, Paul did not do this. Instead, he showed them Christ in at least twenty different aspects for their enjoyment. Paul saw that the enjoyment of Christ through the work of the cross solves all the problems in the church. The killing of Christ's death is included in Him. When we enjoy Christ, we also experience His killing death. He is the crucified and resurrected Christ. When we genuinely enjoy Christ, we are terminated. Thus, all the problems in the church life are solved by the genuine enjoyment of Christ as everything.
In the meetings of the church, we share our joy with one another in mutuality. Our meetings are full of joy because our meetings are a fellowship. The testimony of our meeting life is embodied in the first two lines of the chorus of Hymns, #1237, which says, "I'm so happy in this lovely place, / In the garden growing in His grace!" To sing such a song is difficult when we are alone in our living room. But when we are enjoying Christ together in the meetings, how rich our enjoyment of Christ is! To enjoy Christ is God's will for us. Therefore, we need to concentrate and focus our entire being on Christ for our enjoyment. In 1 Corinthians Paul's burden was not to focus on any matter other than Christ. Often, there are many matters that can bother us; however, we should have no focus other than Christ. Any person other than Christ will disappoint us. When we focus on Christ for our enjoyment, all the problems in the church life are solved.
ENJOYING CHRIST AS THE TABERNACLE, THE ENTERABLE GOD, AND AS THE REALITY OF ALL THE OFFERINGS OF THE OLD TESTAMENT SO THAT HE MAY BECOME OUR GENUINENESS AND SINCERITY FOR US TO WORSHIP GOD
WITH THE WORSHIP THAT HE SEEKS
We need to enjoy Christ as the tabernacle, the enterable God, and as the reality of all the offerings of the Old Testament so that He may become our genuineness and sincerity for us to worship God with the worship that He seeks (John 1:14; 4:23-24; 14:17a). We enjoy Christ, not as the unapproachable God, but as the enterable God. As we enjoy Him in such a way, He becomes in us the reality of all the offerings of the Old Testament. Thus, He becomes our genuineness and sincerity for us to worship God with the worship that the Father seeks.
The Father is a seeker-一One who is seeking true worshippers (John 4:23). The worship of the Father should not be an afterthought to us at the Lord's table meeting, nor should we limit our worship of the Father to one time a week; instead, we should worship the Father throughout the day. The Lord Jesus told the Samaritan woman that the true worshippers worship the Father in spirit and in truthfulness. Truthfulness is our enjoyment of Christ as the reality of all the Old Testament offerings for our application, experience, and enjoyment. When we enjoy Christ daily as the Spirit of reality—the pneumatic Christ-- He, as the reality of all the offerings, becomes our genuineness and sincerity for the true worship of God.
Christ as the Fulfillment of the Tabernacle and All the Offerings Being the Reality and Content of the Universe
Christ as the fulfillment of the tabernacle and all the offerings is the reality and content of the universe. The tabernacle, as an accurate and detailed picture of God's economy in the universe, portrays our detailed experiences of Christ so that Christ can be wrought into us and we can be built into Him for His corporate expression. This is the reality and content of the universe. By the tabernacle and the offerings, we become a mutual abode with the Triune God. He dwells in us and we dwell in Him. The consummate mutual abode is the New Jerusalem. We are the tabernacle and God is dwelling in us; He is the temple and we are dwelling in Him. We dwell in one another in an eternal coinherence for His glory.
Christ, the Incarnate God, Coming as the Embodiment of God So That Man Could Contact Him and Enter into Him to Enjoy the Riches Contained in God
Christ, the incarnate God, came as the embodiment of God, as illustrated by the tabernacle, so that man could contact Him and enter into Him to enjoy the riches contained in God. The writings of John show us Christ as the fulfillment of the tabernacle and the offerings.
Christ as the Embodiment of God Brought God to Man to Make God Contactable, Touchable, Receivable, Experienceable, Enterable, and Enjoyable
Christ as the embodiment of God brought God to man to make God contactable, touchable, receivable, experienceable, enterable, and enjoyable.
Christ Who Was Incarnated to Be the Living Tabernacle Being Also the Lamb of God, the Aggregate, the Totality, of All the Offerings
Christ who was incarnated to be the living tabernacle is also the Lamb of God, the aggregate, the totality, of all the offerings (John 1:14, 29). In chapter one of John, Christ is both the tabernacle, that we may enter into Him and enjoy all the riches contained in Him as the embodiment of God, and the Lamb of God who is the totality, the aggregate, of all the Old Testament offerings, so that we can enter into God. Christ is now our way to enter into God so that we may enjoy God in the deepest and most intimate way.
As the Tabernacle Christ Being the Enterable God, the Mutual Abode of God and Man, and as the Offerings Being the Way for Us to Enter into God
As the tabernacle Christ is the enterable God, the mutual abode of God and man, and as the offerings He is the way for us to enter into God (John 14:6a). We cannot enter into God except by Christ as the reality of the offerings. In John 14 Thomas said, "Lord, we do not know where You are going; how can we know the way?" (v. 5). Responding to him, Jesus said, WI am the way and the reality and the life; no one comes to the Father except through Me" (v. 6). The meaning of the phrase a I am the way" is that Jesus is the way for us to enter into God. The Lord Jesus is in the Father (v. 10). He is in God and God is in Him, and He wants us to be where He is (17:24). The way for us to daily enter into God and to enjoy the riches contained in God is to take Christ as our way, that is, to take Him as the reality of all the offerings to usher us into Himself as the tabernacle. May this be our daily practice for the rest of our lives.
Christ as the Tabernacle Bringing God to Man and Being Good for Us to Experience, Enter into, and Join to God, and Christ as the Offerings Bringing Man to God So That Man May Be United, Mingled, and Incorporated with God
Christ as the tabernacle brings God to man, and Christ as the offerings brings man to God so that man may be united, mingled, and incorporated with God. The tabernacle is good for us to experience, enter into, and join to God, and the offerings are good for us to enjoy God and mingle with God. At the bronze altar all the offerings were offered up to God. By those offerings and through those offerings the priests entered into the tabernacle. The tabernacle could not be entered through a side door. Likewise, we all must pass through the bronze altar to experience Christ as the reality of all the offerings so that we may enter into God.
The Offerings Being Presents to God by the Appreciators of Christ for Their Intimate Fellowship with Him
The offerings are presents to God by the appreciators of Christ for their intimate fellowship with Him. Leviticus 1 says, "When anyone of you brings an offering to Jehovah…" (v. 2a). The Hebrew word for offering means a present or a gift. The offerings were presents to God. The genuine worship of God is to give Him presents or gifts. We should come to the meetings with presents. These presents or gifts are Christ as the offerings. As we enjoy Christ as the reality of all the offerings, He becomes our genuineness and sincerity. Then we come with the Christ we have experienced to offer Him as a present to God so that He can enjoy us and we can enjoy Him in our fellowship with Him.
Practicing Daily to Take and Apply Christ as Our Sin Offering, Trespass Offering, Burnt Offering, and Meal Offering, Consummating in Our Enjoyment of Him as the Peace Offering Plus the Wave Offering, Heave Offering, and Drink Offering
We daily need to practice taking and applying Christ as our sin offering and trespass offering, which will usher us into the enjoyment of Christ as the burnt offering and the meal offering, consummating in our enjoyment of Him as the peace offering plus the wave offering, heave offering, and drink offering. These are the major offerings in the Old Testament, and Christ is the reality of each of these offerings. If we apply Christ in all these aspects, He will become more precious, more beautiful, and dearer to us; thus, we will love Him to the uttermost. In the diagram of the tabernacle, the first item is the bronze altar where the offerings were presented to God. In order to enter the tabernacle, one had to start at the bronze altar. In the same way, we must take Christ as all the offerings in order to begin our journey into the Triune God.
Every day we need to move out of ourselves and travel into the Triune God. In the tabernacle, the center of the divine administration is the incense altar which typifies Christ's interceding life as the divine "motor" in God's economy. Christ in His life of intercessory prayer, typified by the incense altar, is the divine "motor" which makes all the offerings effective to us in our experience. The way to take and apply all the offerings is by prayer.
The Sin Offering Signifying That Christ Was Made Sin for Us That through His Death on the Cross, Sin Might Be Condemned
In Leviticus 1, the order of the offerings begins with the burnt offering (v. 3). However, in our experience, the trespass offering and the sin offering come first (see Experiencing Christ as the Offerings for the Church Meetings, p. 69). The sin offering signifies that Christ was made sin for us that through His death on the cross, sin might be condemned (Lev. 4:3; 2 Cor. 5:21; Rom. 8:3; John 1:29; 3:14). Christ was made sin on our behalf. He took away the sin of the world. When Christ was crucified, He was in the likeness of the flesh of sin, but He in no way participated in the sin of the flesh. He was pure, He was perfect, and He was sinless. He was crucified in the likeness of the flesh of sin, and He was the real sin offering.
In John 3, with the case of Nicodemus, we see Christ as the sin offering. Outwardly, Nicodemus was a good man, a moral man, and an ethical man. But Christ spoke to him concerning the bronze serpent that was lifted up in the wilderness (v. 14). As the Son of Man, Christ also needed to be lifted up that everyone who believes into Him would have the serpentine nature— the sin nature一 annulled and that they would have eternal life (v. 15). This is Christ as the sin offering. It does not matter how good people may look outwardly. Every man is full of sin. Psalm 51:5 says, "Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, / And in sin did my mother conceive me." Each morning when we rise from our sleep, we can say, "Lord, I take You as my sin offering." Just by praying such a short prayer, the law of life is activated within us. We may continue by saying, "Lord, thank You that You are my sin offering. I exercise my spirit of faith to take You and apply You as my sin offering right now." Such a prayer will usher us into God.
The Trespass Offering Signifying That Christ Bore Our Sins in His Own Body and Was Judged by God on the Cross to Deal with Our Sinful Deeds That We Might Be Forgiven in Our Sinful Conduct
The trespass offering signifies that Christ bore our sins in His own body and was judged by God on the cross to deal with our sinful deeds that we might be forgiven in our sinful conduct (Lev. 5:6; 1 Pet. 2:24; 3:18; Isa. 53:5-6, 10-11; John 4:15-18). Christ bore up our sins in His body on the tree. This is not referring to the sin nature, but rather to the sinful acts that come out of that sin nature. Sin in the singular number refers to the poisonous nature of Satan within man. Sins in the plural number refer to the conduct, the sinful acts, which come out of the sin nature. We need Christ every day as our trespass offering.
In John 4 with the case of the Samaritan woman, we can see Christ as the trespass offering. The Lord showed her that He was the living water (w. 10, 13-14), and she responded by saying, a Sir, give me this water so that I will not thirst nor come here to draw." But the Lord said to her, "Go, call your husband and come here." Then she said, a I do not have a husband." Jesus then said to her, "You have well said, I do not have a husband, for you have had five husbands, and the one you now have is not your husband" (vv. 15-18). The Lord confessed her sins for her. She contacted Him, and He confessed her sins for her.
That confession is what enabled her to drink of the living water. She could not have taken a drink of the living water, if she had not enjoyed Christ as her trespass offering. We need to enjoy Christ as our trespass offering every day. We need to come into the light, but we do not need to be introspective. We simply need to contact the Lord, preferably in the morning, which is one of the best times. We can say, "Lord, I take You as my sin offering. I love You and I open to You. Thank You that You are also my trespass offering.K Then the Lord may shine on us concerning some things. In the light we say, "Lord, forgive me. Forgive me for my thoughts about that brother. Forgive me for my reaction to that sister." While we are confessing, we enjoy a glorious "trespass- offering shower"! The blood washes us and we are ushered into God. When we enjoy Christ as our trespass offering, we are digging away all the dirt out of our being so that Christ as the indwelling spring can gush up within us (v. 14).
May this be our daily practice. If it is, then the enjoyment of Christ will increase in the church life. When we say, "Lord, I take You as my trespass offering," all of our condemnation and guilt go away. We need to confess many things throughout the day. Whenever something comes up, we should enjoy Christ as our trespass offering. This is like washing our hands. We should take a "sin and trespass-offering shower" each morning, and then during the day we "wash our hands" by confessing our sins. If something happens, immediately we can say, "O Lord, I confess." This is to "wash our hands."
The Burnt Offering Signifying Christ as God's Pleasure and Satisfaction, as the One Whose Living on Earth Was Absolutely for God
The burnt offering, which was wholly for God's satisfaction, signifies Christ as God's pleasure and satisfaction, as the One whose living on earth was absolutely for God (Lev. 1:3; Num. 28:2-3; John 7:16-18), Christ is God's pleasure; He is God's satisfaction. He is absolute for God. Do not think that you are absolute. Peter considered that he was absolute (John 13:37). He said to the Lord, "Even if I must die with You, I will by no means deny You" (Matt. 26:35). This was Peter's natural confidence. However, his natural confidence, natural strength, and natural absoluteness were all broken through his failure (vv. 69-75). Failures are a mercy to us. Peter's failure was a great mercy to him. Eventually, the Lord said to Peter, "Simon, son of John, do you love Me more than these?" (John 21:15a). The Lord asked him whether or not he loved Him more than the other brothers. Peter answered, "Yes, Lord, You know that I love You" (v. 15b). He had no more confidence in himself. He realized that he was not absolute. He realized that he needed Christ to be his burnt offering, his absoluteness. Each morning we can pray, "Lord, I take You as my burnt offering.w When we pray such a prayer, we identify with Him, we become one with Him, and He becomes our absoluteness.
We can also see the burnt offering in John 7. Verse 16 says, "Jesus therefore answered them and said, My teaching is not Mine, but His who sent Me." The Lord did not speak from Himself. Verse 18 continues, "He who speaks from himself seeks his own glory," The Lord was absolute not to speak His own word or to seek His own glory.
The Meal Offering Signifying Christ in His Humanity and in His Human Living, Which Was Proper, Even, Tender, Fine, Balanced, Pure, and Sinless
The meal offering signifies Christ in His humanity and in His human living, which was proper, even, tender, fine, balanced, pure, and sinless (Lev. 2:1; John 7:46; 18:38; 19:4, 6). Every day we need to say, "Lord, I take You as my meal offering." His humanity is tender, fine, balanced, pure, and sinless. We can partake of His sinless humanity when we take Him as our meal offering. The meal offering is fine flour mingled, soaked, saturated, and permeated with oil (Lev. 2:4); it has no leaven or honey (v. 11). Leaven brings in corruption and honey brings in rottenness. In Christ as the meal offering, there is no leaven or honey. No leaven means to have nothing corrupt, evil, dark, or sinful. Honey refers to our natural affection.
An element of leaven is ambition. In Christ there was no leaven of ambition. He desired only to carry out the Fathers will. In the recent rebellion, a number of brothers were full of ambition and also full of natural affection. As a result of the "honey" of natural affection, the contamination of death spread easily among them. We must be aware of this so that we can avoid such a result. We need to take Christ as our meal offering, saying, "Lord, I take You as my meal offering. I take You as my sinless supply. I take You as the One who has no ambition, no leaven, and no natural affection."
Christ as our meal offering is so fine. When He fed the five thousand, He was so proper and balanced. He had the people sit down in an orderly fashion, in groups of fifties and hundreds (Mark 6:40). After the feast was over, He told His disciples to pick up all the fragments (John 6:12-13). This displays the Lord's fine humanity. We might have said, "Lord, all of these fragments of bones and bread are biodegradable. We do not need to pick them up." But the Lord was so fine; He had His disciples gather up all of the fragments. Perhaps, in the morning, the young people may consider that they do need to make up their beds because they will just get back into their beds later in the evening. However, we need to take Christ as our meal offering, even in making our bed. We should not make our beds in ourselves; we should pray, "Lord, I take You as my meal offering. I want to enjoy You while I make my bed." Such an orderly bed is a testimony of Christ as the meal offering. When the Lord resurrected He did not explode out of the grave throwing everything in all directions. Instead, He folded up the handkerchief that was over His head (20:7). Later, when Peter and John came to the tomb and saw it (w. 4-6), the handkerchief and the linen cloths were a testimony to them that the Lord had resurrected.
The Peace Offering Signifying Christ as the Peacemaker Enabling Us to Enjoy Christ with God and to Have Fellowship with God in Christ for Our Mutual Satisfaction with God
The peace offering signifies Christ as the Peacemaker, the One who became the peace and the fellowship between God and us by shedding His blood and dying for us, enabling us to enjoy Christ with God and to have fellowship with God in Christ for our mutual satisfaction with God (Lev. 3:1; Eph. 2:14-15; John 12:1-3; 20:21; Rev. 21:2). Every day we need to say, "Lord, I take You as my peace offering." In John 12 there was a vital group meeting in Simon the lepers home (Mark 14:3), enjoying Christ as the peace offering. Martha was serving, Lazarus was testifying, and Mary was loving (John 12:2-3). In that outward environment of affliction (Bethany means house of affliction), they enjoyed Christ as their peace offering. Eventually, the New Jerusalem will be the ultimate, universal feast of the peace offering.
The Wave Offering Signifying the Resurrected Christ in Love
The wave offering signifies the resurrected Christ in love (Lev. 7:30; 10:15), We can tell the Lord in the morning, KI take You as my wave offering. I want to enjoy You as the resurrected One today in love. I want to love You in resurrection, and I want my love for everyone to be in resurrection, not in my natural life." This offering was waved, typifying Christ as the resurrected One. He is living, He is waving, He is moving, and He is not stagnant.
The Heave Offering Signifying the Powerful Christ in Ascension and Exaltation
The heave offering signifies the powerful Christ in ascension and exaltation (Lev. 7:32; Exo. 29:27; Eph. 1:21). The heave offering was the shoulder of the offering that the serving priests heaved up before the Lord on the altar. This signifies Christ as the ascended One in exaltation. We can pray this simple prayer: "Lord, I take You by faith and apply You right now as my heave offering."
The Drink Offering Signifying Christ as the Enjoyment of the Offerer, Enabling the Offerer to Be Filled with Christ as the Heavenly Wine and Even to Become the Wine Offered to God for His Enjoyment and Satisfaction
The drink offering signifies Christ as the enjoyment of the offerer, enabling the offerer to be filled with Christ as the heavenly wine and even to become the wine offered to God for His enjoyment and satisfaction (Exo. 29:40; Num. 28:7-10; Isa. 53:12; Phil. 2:17; Judg. 9:13), In 2 Timothy 4:6, Paul said, "For I am already being poured out." He was being poured out as a drink offering. I hope that all of us would become a drink offering. We need to be filled with Christ as the heavenly wine so that we can become wine to God and wine to man. This means that we need to be one with Christ in His sacrificing life. When we enjoy Christ, we become self-sacrificing persons. We become people who are not for themselves. We can say, "Lord, I take You as my drink offering." When we pray such a prayer, we will enjoy a person who is not for Himself, and we will become one with Him in not caring for ourselves. People who are not for themselves are the happiest people. We need to enjoy Christ as our heavenly wine so that we become wine to make God happy and to make man happy, to cheer God and to cheer man, and to be poured out as a drink offering. Eventually, the wine may spill out of our being in martyrdom for God's satisfaction and His people's supply.
LIVING A LIFE ACCORDING TO GOD'S HEART AND WILL BY DAILY ENJOYING CHRIST AS THE REALITY OF ALL OF THE OFFERINGS FOR THE DIVINE GOAL OF THE TRIUNE GOD—THE MUTUAL DWELLING PLACE— HIS UNIVERSAL, ENLARGED, DIVINE-HUMAN INCORPORATION
We need to live a life according to God's heart and will by daily enjoying Christ as the reality of all the offerings for the divine goal of the Triune God, which is to bring us all into Himself that we may take Him as our dwelling place and allow Him to take us as His dwelling place for His universal, enlarged, divine-human incorporation (John 14:23; Rev. 21:3, 22). Every day we need to apply and take Christ as all these offerings, especially in the morning. When we first rise up from sleep, we can say, "Lord, I take You right now as my sin offering and trespass offering. Lord, I open to You. I want to enjoy You as my burnt offering today. Lord, thank You that You are my meal offering. O Lord, be my peace offering. Lord, I want to enjoy You as my wave offering. I take You as my wave offering. I take You as my heave offering. Lord, I also take You as my drink offering." Our prayers do not have to be complicated; rather, they can be very simple. As we are speaking to the Lord, He may stop us. We may say, "O Lord, I am not absolute for You; I fail You all the time." While we are uttering such prayers, we are enjoying Him as the trespass offering. Then we may say, "Lord, I need You as my burnt offering. I cannot make it, but You can make it. I cannot take it, but You can take it J In this way He can be everything to us. Such prayers usher us into God that we may enter into God and God may enter into us, so that we can be His universal, enlarged, divine-human incorporation.—E. M.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|Outline|对照-目录
　
　
第二十篇　我们信心的创始者与成终者
晨兴-大纲|Outline|对照-听抄-目录
读经：来十一1，6，十二1～2；罗十17
壹　我们必须转离其他各种目标，以专一的注意力望断以及于耶稣─来十二1～2，歌一4，诗二七4。
贰　耶稣是信心的创始者，是信心的发起者、开创者、源头和因由─来十二2上：
一　信徒的信实际上不是他们自己的信，乃是基督进到他们里面作他们的信─罗三22与注1，加二16与注1：
１　我们的信是我们对基督的珍赏，作为我们对祂的吸引所起的反应─罗十17。
２　照我们天然的人，我们没有信的能力；我们凭自己没有信。
３　那叫我们得救的信，乃是我们从主所得宝贵的信，就是神所分给的信─彼后一1，西一12。
４　当我们望断以及于耶稣，祂这赐生命的灵（林前十五45下）就将祂自己，将祂信的成分，灌输到我们里面。─引用经文
５　这信不是出于我们自己，乃是出于那将祂自己作信的成分，分赐到我们里面，替我们信者的。
二　信是一种质实的能力，是第六个感官，我们乃是凭这官能，把未见之事或所望之事质实出来，就是把这些事的本质，质成现实─来十一1：
１　质实就是那使我们能把本质实化出来的能力。
２　我们五官的作用是质实外面世界的东西，把一切客观的东西，搬到我们里面来，成为我们主观的经历。
３　就像眼睛对看见，耳朵对听见，鼻子对嗅到一样，信心─我们信心的灵─乃是我们将看不见之属灵世界里的一切，质实到我们里面的器官─林后四13：
a　我们必须运用我们信心的灵，就是我们调和的灵，信并且说我们所经历于主的事。
b　信心是在我们那与圣灵调和的灵里，不是在我们的心思里；怀疑才是在我们的心思里。
４　我们不是顾念、注视所见的，乃是顾念、注视所不见的，因为所见的是暂时的，所不见的才是永远的─林后四18：
a　基督徒的生活是一种未见之事的生活─罗八24～25，来十一27，彼前一8，加六10。
b　召会的堕落是从所不见的事堕落到所见的事。
c　主的恢复是要将祂的召会从所见的事恢复到所不见的事。
５　信心使我们对未见之事有把握，使我们确信所没有看见的事；因此，信是未见之事的证据、证明。
三　信乃是信神是：
１　人非有信，就不能得神的喜悦，不能使神快乐─来十一6上。
２　“到神面前来的人，必须信神是”─来十一6下，直译：
a　信神是，就是信祂是我们的一切，而我们一无所是─约八58，传一2。
b　信神是，含示我们不是；祂必须在凡事上是惟一的一位，独一的一位，我们必须在凡事上什么也不是─来十一5。
c　信神是，就是否认我们的己；在全宇宙中祂是，我们众人什么也不是─路九23。
d　我应当什么都不是；我应当不存在；惟独祂应当存在─“不再是我，乃是基督”─加二20。
e　大数的扫罗悔改转变时，主告诉他说，“我就是…耶稣”─徒九5：
（一）　主的意思是说，“我是那伟大的我是。我是那是的一位。你必须信我是而你不是。”
（二）　至终，扫罗过去了，保罗起来了─徒十三9。
f　这就是信─“哦，何等喜乐，能够一无所有，一无所是，一无所见，惟见在荣耀里活的基督；并且一无所顾，惟顾祂在此地的权益”─达秘。
叁　耶稣是我们信心的成终者、完成者、完全者─来十二2上：
一　我们不断的望断以及于祂，祂就要完成并完全我们奔跑属天赛程所需要的信心─1节。
二　我们望断以及于祂，祂就把天、生命、和力量供应我们，将祂的所是传输并灌注到我们里面，使我们能奔跑属天的赛程，在地上过属天的生活─林后三18。
三　我们所有的信在质的一面都是同样的，但这信的量有多少，乃在于我们有多少接触活的神，使祂得以在我们里面增长─罗十二3:
１　进展阶段的信是借着我们接触三一神而来，祂在我们里面就是信─帖前五17。
２　接受这样的信的路，乃是借着呼求主，向主祷告，并祷读主话，来接触这信的源头，就是主，也就是那经过过程并终极完成的神─来四16，罗十12，提后二22，弗六17～18，来四2。
３　当我们接触祂时，祂就在我们里面涌流，并且我们中间就有相互的信心；我们因着彼此的信心，就同得鼓励─罗一12，门6。
四　我们重生的灵，我们信心的灵，使我们能得胜，胜过撒担所组织并霸占的世界─约壹五4。
五　那无法压制、无法困住之信的大能，推动许多人为主受苦，冒生命的危险，并成为得胜的受差遣者和殉道者，以完成神在信仰里永远的经纶─来十一36～40，路十八8，腓二30，罗十六3～4，徒二十24，提前一4。

TOP-晨兴-大纲|Outline|对照-听抄-目录
　
　
GENERAL SUBJECT
The Crystallization-Study of Epistle to the Hebrews
Message Twenty
The Author and Perfecter of Our Faith
晨兴-纲目|对照-听抄-目录
