Scripture Reading: Heb. 11:1, 6; 12:1-2; Rom. 10:17
I.　We need to look away unto Jesus with undivided attention by turning away from every other object—Heb. 12:1-2; S. S. 1:4; Psa. 27:4.
II.　Jesus is the Author of faith, the Originator, the Inaugurator, the source, and the cause of faith—Heb. 12:2a:
A.　The faith of the believers is actually not their own faith but Christ entering into them to be their faith—Rom. 3:22 and note 1; Gal. 2:16 and note 1:
1.　Our believing is our appreciation of Christ as a reaction to His attraction—Rom. 10:17.
2.　In our natural man we have no believing ability; we do not have faith by ourselves.
3.　The faith by which we are saved is the precious faith that we have received from the Lord, the God-allotted faith—2 Pet. 1:1; Col. 1:12.
4.　When we look away unto Jesus, He as the life-giving Spirit (1 Cor. 15:45b) transfuses us with Himself, His believing element.
5.　This faith is not of ourselves but of Him who imparts Himself as the believing element into us that He may believe for us.
B.　Faith is a substantiating ability, a sixth sense, the sense by which we substantiate, give substance to, the things unseen or hoped for—Heb. 11:1:
1.　Substantiating is the ability which enables us to realize a substance.
2.　The function of our five senses is to substantiate the things of the outside world, transferring all the objective items into us to become our subjective experience.
3.　As the eye is to seeing, the ear to hearing, and the nose to smelling, so faith, our spirit of faith, is the organ whereby we substantiate everything in the unseen spiritual world into us—2 Cor. 4:13:
a.　We must exercise our spirit of faith, our mingled spirit, to believe and to speak the things we have experienced of the Lord.
b.　Faith is in our spirit, which is mingled with the Holy Spirit, not in our mind; doubts are in our mind.
4.　We do not regard, look at, the things which are seen but the things which are not seen; for the things which are seen are temporary, but the things which are not seen are eternal—2 Cor. 4:18:
a.　The Christian life is a life of things unseen—Rom. 8:24-25; Heb. 11:27; 1 Pet. 1:8; Gal. 6:10.
b.　The degradation of the church is the degradation from unseen things to seen things.
c.　The Lord's recovery is to recover His church from things seen to things unseen.
5.　Faith assures us of the things not seen, convincing us of what we do not see; therefore, it is the evidence, the proof, of things unseen.
C.　Faith is to believe that God is:
1.　Without faith it is impossible to please God, to make God happy—Heb. 11:6a.
2.　"He who comes forward to God must believe that He is"—Heb. 11:6b:
a.　To believe that God is, is to believe that He is everything to us and that we are nothing—John 8:58; Eccl.1:2.
b.　To believe that God is implies that we are not; He must be the only One, the unique One, in everything, and we must be nothing in everything—Heb. 11:5.
c.　To believe that God is, is to deny our self; in the whole universe He is, and all of us are nothing—Luke 9:23.
d.　I should not be anything; I should not exist; only He should exist—"It is no longer I, but Christ"—Gal. 2:20.
e.　At his conversion the Lord told Saul of Tarsus, "I am Jesus"—Acts 9:5:
1)　 The Lord was saying, "I am the great I Am. I am the One that is. You must believe that I am and you are not."
2)　 Eventually, Saul was over, and Paul came up—Acts 13:9.
f.　This is faith—"O the joy of having nothing and being nothing, seeing nothing but a living Christ in glory, and being careful for nothing but His interests down here"—J. N. Darby.
III.　Jesus is the Perfecter, the Finisher, the Completer, of our faith—Heb. 12:2a:
A.　As we look away unto Him continually, He will finish and complete the faith that we need for the running of the heavenly race—v. 1.
B.　When we look away unto Him, He ministers heaven, life, and strength to us, transfusing and infusing us with all that He is, that we may be able to run the heavenly race and live the heavenly life on earth—2 Cor. 3:18.
C.　We all have the same faith in quality, but the quantity of faith we have depends upon how much we contact the living God so that we may have Him increased in us—Rom. 12:3:
1.　Faith in the progressing stage comes through our contacting the Triune God, who is faith in us—1 Thes. 5:17.
2.　The way to receive such a faith is to contact its source, the Lord, the processed and consummated God, by calling on Him, praying to Him, and pray-reading His word—Heb. 4:16; Rom. 10:12; 2 Tim. 2:22; Eph. 6:17-18; Heb. 4:2.
3.　When we contact Him, He is overflowing within us, and there is a mutuality of faith among us; we are encouraged through the faith which is in one another—Rom. 1:12; Philem. 6.
D.　Our regenerated spirit, our spirit of faith, is the victory that overcomes the Satan-organized-and-usurped world—1 John 5:4.
E.　The great irrepressible and unlimited power of faith motivates thousands to suffer for the Lord, risk their lives, and become overcoming sent ones and martyrs for the carrying out of God's eternal economy, which is in faith—Heb. 11:36-40; Luke 18:8; Phil. 2:30; Rom. 16:3-4; Acts 20:24; 1 Tim. 1:4.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|对照-听抄-目录
　
　
第二十篇　我们信心的创始者与成终者
The Author and Perfecter of Our Faith
晨兴-纲目|outline-听抄-目录
读经：来十一1，6，十二1～2；罗十17
壹　我们必须转离其他各种目标，以专一的注意力望断以及于耶稣─来十二1～2，歌一4，诗二七4。
I.　We need to look away unto Jesus with undivided attention by turning away from every other object—Heb. 12:1-2; S. S. 1:4; Psa. 27:4.
贰　耶稣是信心的创始者，是信心的发起者、开创者、源头和因由─来十二2上：
II.　Jesus is the Author of faith, the Originator, the Inaugurator, the source, and the cause of faith—Heb. 12:2a:
一　信徒的信实际上不是他们自己的信，乃是基督进到他们里面作他们的信─罗三22与注1，加二16与注1：
A.　The faith of the believers is actually not their own faith but Christ entering into them to be their faith—Rom. 3:22 and note 1; Gal. 2:16 and note 1:
１　我们的信是我们对基督的珍赏，作为我们对祂的吸引所起的反应─罗十17。
1.　Our believing is our appreciation of Christ as a reaction to His attraction—Rom. 10:17.
２　照我们天然的人，我们没有信的能力；我们凭自己没有信。
2.　In our natural man we have no believing ability; we do not have faith by ourselves.
３　那叫我们得救的信，乃是我们从主所得宝贵的信，就是神所分给的信─彼后一1，西一12。
3.　The faith by which we are saved is the precious faith that we have received from the Lord, the God-allotted faith—2 Pet. 1:1; Col. 1:12.
４　当我们望断以及于耶稣，祂这赐生命的灵（林前十五45下）就将祂自己，将祂信的成分，灌输到我们里面。─引用经文
4.　When we look away unto Jesus, He as the life-giving Spirit (1 Cor. 15:45b) transfuses us with Himself, His believing element.
５　这信不是出于我们自己，乃是出于那将祂自己作信的成分，分赐到我们里面，替我们信者的。
5.　This faith is not of ourselves but of Him who imparts Himself as the believing element into us that He may believe for us.
二　信是一种质实的能力，是第六个感官，我们乃是凭这官能，把未见之事或所望之事质实出来，就是把这些事的本质，质成现实─来十一1：
B.　Faith is a substantiating ability, a sixth sense, the sense by which we substantiate, give substance to, the things unseen or hoped for—Heb. 11:1:
１　质实就是那使我们能把本质实化出来的能力。
1.　Substantiating is the ability which enables us to realize a substance.
２　我们五官的作用是质实外面世界的东西，把一切客观的东西，搬到我们里面来，成为我们主观的经历。
2.　The function of our five senses is to substantiate the things of the outside world, transferring all the objective items into us to become our subjective experience.
３　就像眼睛对看见，耳朵对听见，鼻子对嗅到一样，信心─我们信心的灵─乃是我们将看不见之属灵世界里的一切，质实到我们里面的器官─林后四13：
3.　As the eye is to seeing, the ear to hearing, and the nose to smelling, so faith, our spirit of faith, is the organ whereby we substantiate everything in the unseen spiritual world into us—2 Cor. 4:13:
a　我们必须运用我们信心的灵，就是我们调和的灵，信并且说我们所经历于主的事。
a.　We must exercise our spirit of faith, our mingled spirit, to believe and to speak the things we have experienced of the Lord.
b　信心是在我们那与圣灵调和的灵里，不是在我们的心思里；怀疑才是在我们的心思里。
b.　Faith is in our spirit, which is mingled with the Holy Spirit, not in our mind; doubts are in our mind.
４　我们不是顾念、注视所见的，乃是顾念、注视所不见的，因为所见的是暂时的，所不见的才是永远的─林后四18：
4.　We do not regard, look at, the things which are seen but the things which are not seen; for the things which are seen are temporary, but the things which are not seen are eternal—2 Cor. 4:18:
a　基督徒的生活是一种未见之事的生活─罗八24～25，来十一27，彼前一8，加六10。
a.　The Christian life is a life of things unseen—Rom. 8:24-25; Heb. 11:27; 1 Pet. 1:8; Gal. 6:10.
b　召会的堕落是从所不见的事堕落到所见的事。
b.　The degradation of the church is the degradation from unseen things to seen things.
c　主的恢复是要将祂的召会从所见的事恢复到所不见的事。
c.　The Lord's recovery is to recover His church from things seen to things unseen.
５　信心使我们对未见之事有把握，使我们确信所没有看见的事；因此，信是未见之事的证据、证明。
5.　Faith assures us of the things not seen, convincing us of what we do not see; therefore, it is the evidence, the proof, of things unseen.
三　信乃是信神是：
C.　Faith is to believe that God is:
１　人非有信，就不能得神的喜悦，不能使神快乐─来十一6上。
1.　Without faith it is impossible to please God, to make God happy—Heb. 11:6a.
２　“到神面前来的人，必须信神是”─来十一6下，直译：
2.　"He who comes forward to God must believe that He is"—Heb. 11:6b:
a　信神是，就是信祂是我们的一切，而我们一无所是─约八58，传一2。
a.　To believe that God is, is to believe that He is everything to us and that we are nothing—John 8:58; Eccl.1:2.
b　信神是，含示我们不是；祂必须在凡事上是惟一的一位，独一的一位，我们必须在凡事上什么也不是─来十一5。
b.　To believe that God is implies that we are not; He must be the only One, the unique One, in everything, and we must be nothing in everything—Heb. 11:5.
c　信神是，就是否认我们的己；在全宇宙中祂是，我们众人什么也不是─路九23。
c.　To believe that God is, is to deny our self; in the whole universe He is, and all of us are nothing—Luke 9:23.
d　我应当什么都不是；我应当不存在；惟独祂应当存在─“不再是我，乃是基督”─加二20。
d.　I should not be anything; I should not exist; only He should exist—"It is no longer I, but Christ"—Gal. 2:20.
e　大数的扫罗悔改转变时，主告诉他说，“我就是…耶稣”─徒九5：
e.　At his conversion the Lord told Saul of Tarsus, "I am Jesus"—Acts 9:5:
（一）　主的意思是说，“我是那伟大的我是。我是那是的一位。你必须信我是而你不是。”
1)　 The Lord was saying, "I am the great I Am. I am the One that is. You must believe that I am and you are not."
（二）　至终，扫罗过去了，保罗起来了─徒十三9。
2)　 Eventually, Saul was over, and Paul came up—Acts 13:9.
f　这就是信─“哦，何等喜乐，能够一无所有，一无所是，一无所见，惟见在荣耀里活的基督；并且一无所顾，惟顾祂在此地的权益”─达秘。
f.　This is faith—"O the joy of having nothing and being nothing, seeing nothing but a living Christ in glory, and being careful for nothing but His interests down here"—J. N. Darby.
叁　耶稣是我们信心的成终者、完成者、完全者─来十二2上：
III.　Jesus is the Perfecter, the Finisher, the Completer, of our faith—Heb. 12:2a:
一　我们不断的望断以及于祂，祂就要完成并完全我们奔跑属天赛程所需要的信心─1节。
A.　As we look away unto Him continually, He will finish and complete the faith that we need for the running of the heavenly race—v. 1.
二　我们望断以及于祂，祂就把天、生命、和力量供应我们，将祂的所是传输并灌注到我们里面，使我们能奔跑属天的赛程，在地上过属天的生活─林后三18。
B.　When we look away unto Him, He ministers heaven, life, and strength to us, transfusing and infusing us with all that He is, that we may be able to run the heavenly race and live the heavenly life on earth—2 Cor. 3:18.
三　我们所有的信在质的一面都是同样的，但这信的量有多少，乃在于我们有多少接触活的神，使祂得以在我们里面增长─罗十二3:
C.　We all have the same faith in quality, but the quantity of faith we have depends upon how much we contact the living God so that we may have Him increased in us—Rom. 12:3:
１　进展阶段的信是借着我们接触三一神而来，祂在我们里面就是信─帖前五17。
1.　Faith in the progressing stage comes through our contacting the Triune God, who is faith in us—1 Thes. 5:17.
２　接受这样的信的路，乃是借着呼求主，向主祷告，并祷读主话，来接触这信的源头，就是主，也就是那经过过程并终极完成的神─来四16，罗十12，提后二22，弗六17～18，来四2。
2.　The way to receive such a faith is to contact its source, the Lord, the processed and consummated God, by calling on Him, praying to Him, and pray-reading His word—Heb. 4:16; Rom. 10:12; 2 Tim. 2:22; Eph. 6:17-18; Heb. 4:2.
３　当我们接触祂时，祂就在我们里面涌流，并且我们中间就有相互的信心；我们因着彼此的信心，就同得鼓励─罗一12，门6。
3.　When we contact Him, He is overflowing within us, and there is a mutuality of faith among us; we are encouraged through the faith which is in one another—Rom. 1:12; Philem. 6.
四　我们重生的灵，我们信心的灵，使我们能得胜，胜过撒担所组织并霸占的世界─约壹五4。
D.　Our regenerated spirit, our spirit of faith, is the victory that overcomes the Satan-organized-and-usurped world—1 John 5:4.
五　那无法压制、无法困住之信的大能，推动许多人为主受苦，冒生命的危险，并成为得胜的受差遣者和殉道者，以完成神在信仰里永远的经纶─来十一36～40，路十八8，腓二30，罗十六3～4，徒二十24，提前一4。
E.　The great irrepressible and unlimited power of faith motivates thousands to suffer for the Lord, risk their lives, and become overcoming sent ones and martyrs for the carrying out of God's eternal economy, which is in faith—Heb. 11:36-40; Luke 18:8; Phil. 2:30; Rom. 16:3-4; Acts 20:24; 1 Tim. 1:4.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|outline-听抄-目录
　
　
MESSAGE TWENTY
THE AUTHOR AND PERFECTER OF OUR FAITH
晨兴-纲目|Outline|对照-目录
文档OCR所得，仅供追求参考
Hebrews 12:1 tells us that as believers we are running a race. To run this race we need to put away every encumbrance, every impediment, every weight, and anything that weighs us down in our pursuit of Christ for the building up of the Body of Christ to consummate the New Jerusalem. We need to put away everything that weighs us down or that is an impediment to our running. A runner in a long-distance race never runs with a backpack or with heavy shoes. His clothing is streamlined. He puts away every encumbrance. We also need to put away every encumbrance, especially the sin which so easily entangles us, and continue running the race. If a runner falls in a race, he does not stop and condemn himself because he fell; he just gets up and continues to run.
When we fall we have Christ as our trespass offering. We have the blood of the eternal covenant. Because we have the bequest of the forgiveness of sins, we can enter into the Holy of Holies and continue running the Christian race. The race that we are running is not a sprint but a long-distance race; it will last the course of our entire Christian life. Hence, we need to run with endurance the race which is set before us.
LOOKING AWAY UNTO JESUS WITH UNDIVIDED ATTENTION BY TURNING AWAY FROM EVERY OTHER OBJECT
We need to look away unto Jesus with undivided attention by turning away from every other object (Heb. 12:1-2; S. S, 1:4; Psa. 27:4). We run the race set before us by looking away unto Jesus. The chorus of Hymns, #1206 says, "Look away! O look away! / Look to Jesus now today! / Look away from everything unto Jesus, / Look away from everything to Him!" We must look away from everything to Jesus. We must look away from failing self. We must look away from all strife and confusion. We must look away from our environment and our circumstances. We must look away from our failures. We must look away from our fallen selves. Looking at ourselves never encourages us. We must look away unto Jesus. He is the greatest attraction in the universe (Heb. 12:2 and note 2 ). He is like an immense magnet, drawing all His seekers to Himself. If we get close to Him, we will be drawn to Him, and we will be ruined and wrecked for Him.
Song of Songs 1:4 says, "Draw me; we will run after you." When we look away unto Jesus He draws us to Himself. His drawing power is our pursuing power. Actually, His drawing us is our pursuing. This is the reason that we need to pray, "Lord, draw me." In Psalm 27:4 the psalmist said, "One thing I have asked from Jehovah; / That do I seek: / To dwell in the house of Jehovah / All the days of my life, / To behold the loveliness of Jehovah, / And to inquire in His temple." The phrase to inquire in His temple relates to the experience of Christ as the incense altar. As we behold His loveliness and beauty, His feelings and burdens are infused into us. We then become one with Him in His life of intercessory prayer, and we inquire of Him. His prayer becomes our prayer, and we inquire of Him for His interests on the earth. What a supreme enjoyment this is!
Jesus never disappoints us. Our gaze should be continually upon Him. We should not look at anyone or anything else. The church life is all about looking away unto Jesus. It is about gazing on this person. It is about our being infused and transformed with and by the person of Christ to become Christ in a corporate way for His glory. If we expect anything in the church life other than Jesus, we will be disappointed. In the past some came into the recovery with dreams and expectations of becoming something. They were eventually disappointed. Therefore, we should pray, "Lord, rescue me from my ambition to be somebody in Your recovery.w There is nothing in the recovery but Jesus. He is everything, and He is the reality of the hundreds of bequests in the New Testament.
JESUS BEING THE AUTHOR OF FAITH, THE ORIGINATOR,
THE INAUGURATOR, THE SOURCE, AND THE CAUSE OF FAITH
Jesus is the Author of faith, the Originator, the Inaugurator, the source, and the cause of faith (Heb. 12:2a). Faith is not of ourselves. It is impossible to believe in our natural man. In our natural man we do not have the believing ability. Jesus is the Author of our faith. He begets faith in us; hence, He is the source of faith, the cause of faith.
The Faith of the Believers Being Not Their Own Faith but Christ Entering into Them to Be Their Faith
The faith of the believers is actually not their own faith but Christ entering into them to be their faith. Both Romans and Galatians speak about our being justified by the faith of Jesus Christ (Rom. 3:22 and note 1; Gal. 2:16 and note 1). The faith is His faith. It is Christ as our faith. I am so grateful to the Lord that He came into me and became my faith, my believing ability. We could not believe, but He has come into our spirit as our believing ability.
Our Believing Being Our Appreciation of Christ as a Reaction to His Attraction
Our believing is our appreciation of Christ as a reaction to His attraction (Rom. 10:17). When we speak to unbelievers, we need to present the beautiful Christ to them. We must be like the jade salesmen. When a jade salesman displays his jade, he infuses you with the good qualities of the jade. Once you see the beauty of the jade, this causes you to appreciate it. Before you know it, the jade is in your pocket and your money is with the jade salesman. In the same way, when we preach the gospel, we must help people to look away unto Jesus. If they can look at Jesus, they will be attracted. Then an appreciation for Jesus will be stirred up within them. This appreciation is a reaction to His attraction. That reaction is faith. Eventually, they will say," I believe!"
I can testify that this happened to me. At the time I got saved, I looked at the person of Jesus, and I appreciated Him. Eventually, I believed. Today people often look at the fallen situation in Christendom; they see the hypocrisy and other evil things mixed together with Christ, and they reject Him. This is the reason that we need to present the pure, high, uplifted, and wonderful Christ to people. When they appreciate Him, there will be a reaction to His attraction, and they will believe. Romans 10:17 says, "So faith comes out of hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ." When we speak the word to people, faith is begotten in them.
Our Natural Man Having No Believing Ability
In our natural man we have no believing ability; we do not have faith by ourselves. Because we have no believing ability in our natural man, we need Christ as the Author of our faith.
The Faith by Which We Are Saved Being the Precious Faith That We Have Received from the Lord, the God-allotted Faith
The faith by which we are saved is the precious faith that we have received from the Lord, the God-allotted faith (2 Pet. 1:1; Col. 1:12). God has allotted Christ as faith to us. Each of the children of Israel received an assigned portion of the good land for their enjoyment. God allotted Christ as faith to each one of us at the time we received Christ. Now Christ is in us as a lot, an assigned portion, of faith. The "soil of faith" is in our spirit.
Jesus as the Life-giving Spirit Transfusing Us with Himself, His Believing Element
When we look away unto Jesus, He as the life-giving Spirit (1 Cor. 15:45b) transfuses us with Himself, His believing element. By the Lord's mercy, I looked away from so many things when I looked away unto Jesus. I looked away from the questions I had and from the hypocrisy and mixture I observed. There was disappointment everywhere around me. But when I looked at Jesus, the life-giving Spirit transfused me with Himself, and He became my believing element.
This Faith Being Not of Ourselves but of Him Who Imparts Himself as the Believing Element into Us That He May Believe for Us
This faith is not of ourselves but of Him who imparts Himself as the believing element into us that He may believe for us. There are many ways to talk to people about Christ. We can tell them of how He came into us and of what He did for us. When we talk to people about Christ, we need to love them with the love of Christ, and we need to express Christ. The apostle Paul expressed the most excellent divine attributes in his human virtues. Brother Lee said, "His behavior displayed much ascendancy with dignity" (Acts 27:21 and note 2). When people saw him, they saw Christ. We also need to be people who are full of dignity and who have the divine attributes expressed in our human virtues. Hence, what people see is Christ. He is such a marvelous person. As they are looking at Him, He as the life-giving Spirit infuses Himself into them as their believing element to believe for them. Initially, this is the way we receive Christ into us as faith. This is the inception of faith in a believers being.
Christ is not only the Author of our faith, but He is also the Perfecter of our faith. Stephen had the testimony that he was a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit (Acts 6:5). He was full of Christ as faith. We can be such people by continually turning our hearts to the Lord and gazing upon Him (2 Cor. 3:16-18). As we behold the indwelling Christ, He infuses Himself into us as the believing element. As this believing element increases within us, we enjoy and experience Christ as all the bequests in the New Testament and we become a people of full of faith for the carrying out of God's economy.
Faith Being a Substantiating Ability, a Sixth Sense, the Sense by Which We Substantiate, Give Substance to, the Things Unseen or Hoped For
Faith is a substantiating ability, a sixth sense, the sense by which we substantiate, give substance to, the things unseen or hoped for. Faith is our sixth sense. Hebrews 11:1 says, "Now faith is the substantiation of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen," Faith gives us an extra, divine, mystical perception of the things unseen or hoped for. Faith is our substantiating ability.
Substantiating Being the Ability Which Enables Us to Realize a Substance
Substantiating is the ability which enables us to realize a substance. When we open our eyes and exercise our sight, we can substantiate visible things. We have five senses. By these senses, we can hear, see, smell, taste, and touch. If we exercise our sense of touch, we can give substance to a table placed before us. By this sense, the table becomes real to us.
When we first looked away unto Jesus, He as the life-giving Spirit transfused Himself into us as the believing element to believe for us. Faith was transfused into our being, and our mingled spirit became a spirit of faith (2 Cor. 4:13). Faith is now in our spirit. Doubts are in our mind, but faith is in our spirit. Our spirit of faith is a sixth sense by which we can substantiate the unseen God, the unseen Christ, and the unseen things of His economy.
Today through our spirit of faith, our sixth sense, we are substantiating the invisible things. Though we do not see Christ with our physical eyes (1 Pet. l:7b-8), we can testify that we do see Him. He is more real to us than all of the visible things. But if we do not exercise our spirit, we will be like the unbelievers. Therefore, we need to exercise our spirit and use our sixth sense. This sixth sense functions like our five senses. According to the Bible, by exercising our spirit of faith, our sixth sense, we can see God (Matt. 5:8; 2 Cor. 3:18), hear God (Rev. 2:7), smell God (2 Cor. 2:15), taste God (1 Pet. 2:3), and touch God (1 John 1:1). What a miracle this is!
The Function of Our Five Senses Being to Substantiate the Things of the Outside World
The function of our five senses is to substantiate the things of the outside world, transferring all the objective items into us to become our subjective experience. All the things of Christ in the divine and mystical realm, including all the bequests of the New Testament, are substantiated when we exercise our spirit of faith. When we substantiate these things, bringing them into our being, they become our subjective experience.
Our Spirit of Faith Being the Organ Whereby We Substantiate Everything in the Unseen Spiritual World into Us
As the eye is to seeing, the ear to hearing, and the nose to smelling, so faith, our spirit of faith, is the organ whereby we substantiate everything in the unseen spiritual world into us (2 Cor. 4:13).
Exercising Our Spirit of Faith to Believe and to Speak the Things We Have Experienced of the Lord
We must exercise our spirit of faith, our mingled spirit, to believe and to speak the things we have experienced of the Lord. The enemy would like to tell us that our experience is not real. But we need to exercise our spirit of faith to believe the things we have experienced. Also, when we speak, the main thing is to exercise our spirit of faith.
Faith Being in Our Spirit
Faith is in our spirit, which is mingled with the Holy Spirit, not in our mind; doubts are in our mind. When we come to the Word, we need to exercise our spirit of faith to receive revelation and to see God, touch God, taste God, smell God, and hear God.
The Things Seen Being Temporary, but the Things Not Seen Being Eternal
We do not regard, look at, the things which are seen but the things which are not seen; for the things which are seen are temporary, but the things which are not seen are eternal (2 Cor. 4:18). We need to spend our days looking at the unseen things of the divine and mystical realm. We are not looking at the seen things. This is the reason that we do not lose heart. The outer man is decaying; that is seen. Yet the inner man is being renewed day by day; this is unseen. Our momentary lightness of affliction is something that is seen, but the eternal weight of glory being worked into our being is something unseen. Likewise, the local churches as the procedure to arrive at the goal of God's economy, the reality of the Body of Christ, are ninety percent physical. Hence, they are mainly in the realm of what can be seen. We love and treasure the local churches as the procedure. But the procedure is to bring us to the goal of the organic Body of Christ, which is in the divine and mystical realm; hence, the Body is in the realm of the unseen things.
The Christian Life Being a Life of Things Unseen
The Christian life is a life of things unseen (Rom. 8:24-25; 1 Pet. 1:8; Gal. 6:10). Moses persevered as seeing the unseen One (Heb. 11:27), As the son of Pharaoh's daughter, he had everything. But by faith, he left Egypt because he realized that all the wealth of Egypt was refuse. By the sixth sense, he saw the unseen One and His unsearchable riches and glory.
The Degradation of the Church Being the Degradation from Unseen Things to Seen Things, and the Lord's Recovery Being to Recover His Church from Things Seen to Things Unseen
The degradation of the church is the degradation from unseen things to seen things. In fallen Christendom many like to bring the unseen things into the realm of things seen. For this reason, there are many idols. This is the degradation of the church. But we also must be careful. As the things of God's economy are ministered, we may have the thought that many of these things are too high and impractical. We certainly need a practical expression of the church, but we should not say that the things of God's economy are too high. To say such things may indicate that we are in the realm of things seen and not in the realm of the unseen things. Brother Lees ministry was a ministry of the unseen things of God's economy. The Lord's recovery is to recover His church from things seen to things unseen.
Faith Assuring Us of the Things Not Seen
Faith assures us of the things not seen, convincing us of what we do not see; therefore, it is the evidence, the proof, of things unseen.
Faith Being to Believe That God Is
Being Impossible to Please God without Faith
Faith is to believe that God is. Without faith it is impossible to please God, to make God happy (Heb. 11:6a). Every day we can make God happy by exercising our spirit of faith to substantiate Him by tasting Him, seeing Him, hearing Him, touching Him, and smelling Him.
Coming Forward to God, Believing That He Is
To Believe That God Is, Being to Believe That He Is Everything to Us and That We Are Nothing
"He who comes forward to God must believe that He is" (Heb. 11:6b). To believe that God is, is to believe that He is everything to us and that we are nothing (John 8:58; Eccl. 1:2). When we exercise our spirit of faith to touch God, to see God, to hear God, to smell God, to taste God, to be infused with God, and to substantiate God, He becomes everything and we become nothing; He is and we are not. He is the "I Am," (John 8:58). "I Am" is the name of the Triune God (Exo, 3:14), He is the present tense. In the whole universe, nothing else is; only He is. When people see us, they see Christ. He is the One who is. Eventually, what is seen will not be. Only God is, and only He remains forever. Today He is dispensing Himself into our being, and we are becoming Him (not in the Godhead), the corporate One who is. This is the New Jerusalem.
To Believe That God Is Implying That We Are Not
To believe that God is implies that we are not; He must be the only One, the unique One, in everything, and we must be nothing in everything (Heb. 11:5). When we exercise our spirit of faith, we make Him the only One in everything, and we become nothing in everything. Hebrews 11:5-6 says, "By faith Enoch was translated so that he should not see death; and he was not found, because God had translated him. For before his translation he obtained the testimony that he had been well pleasing to God. But without faith it is impossible to be well pleasing to Him, for he who comes forward to God must believe that He is and that He is a rewarder of those who diligently seek Him." May the Lord put such an aspiration in us so that we pray, "Lord, I want to obtain the testimony of being well pleasing to You." What really matters is that we are well pleasing to God.
Genesis 5:24 says, "And Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him." Enoch lived God, enjoyed God, and substantiated God by faith. He made God the One who is, and he became the one who was not. He was "not" in his whole being, and God became "is" (the verb to be) in Enoch's whole being. Eventually, Enoch was translated. He walked with God until he walked into the heavenly Holy of Holies. He was "not" because he lived a life of faith to make God the only One who "is."
To Believe That God Is, Being to Deny Our Self
To believe that God is, is to deny our self; in the whole universe He is, and all of us are nothing (Luke 9:23). When we exercise our sixth sense, our spirit of faith, to substantiate God, we make Him the only One who is. We become nothing. It is our joy to be nothing. It is our joy that He would be the One who is. We even need to pray, "Lord, I do not want to be anything. I do not want to be anybody. I want You to be my everything. You are and I am not." Even in our family life, in our daily life, a husband should say, "Lord, I cannot be a proper husband.
I am absolutely nothing, Lord. I do not know how to be a husband. I need You to be a husband in me and through me to my wife." This is the blessing of denying ourselves in everything.
Only Christ Should Exist
I should not be anything; I should not exist; only He should exist- "It is no longer I...but...Christ" (Gal. 2:20). We must exercise our spirit to believe that God is. This means that we are nothing. We should not exist, and He should be the existing One. This is the meaning of the phrase "It is no longer I...but...Christ."
"I Am Jesus"
At his conversion the Lord told Saul of Tarsus, "I am Jesus" (Acts 9:5). The Lord was saying, a I am the great I Am. I am the One that is. You must believe that I am and you are not." Saul of Tarsus was terminated on the road to Damascus. Eventually, Saul was over, and Paul came up (13:9). In his experience he touched the great "I Am." The great "I Am" became the One who is to him. Christ was everything to him in all things, and he was nothing in all things. With Saul of Tarsus before his conversion it was "I," "me," "my," and "mine." But after his conversion it became "He," "Him," "His," and "Christ."
God is and we are not, because we are terminated. The first stanza of Hymns, #483 says:
Buried with Christ, and raised Him with too;
What is there left for me to do?
Simply to cease from struggling and strife,
Simply to walk in newness of life.
Glory be to God!
Since Christ is the only One who is, and He becomes everything in us, we become Christ (1 Cor. 12:12), Our living is Christ (Phil. 1:21). When others see us, they see Christ.
Faith Being the Joy of Having Nothing, Being Nothing, Seeing Nothing but a Living Christ in Glory and Being Careful for Nothing but His Interests
This is faith— "O the joy of having nothing and being nothing, seeing nothing but a living Christ in glory, and being careful for nothing but His interests down here" (J. N. Darby). At the end of Brother Lee's life, he really loved and treasured this quote from John Nelson Darby. It would be good if we prayed over this, saying, a Lord, I want the joy of having nothing but You. I do not want to hold on to anything but You. I want the joy of being nothing but You. Lord, I want the joy of seeing nothing but You, and I want the joy of caring for nothing but Your interests, for the carrying out Your economy." This is faith. This is to make God the only One who is in our being and to make us nothing in everything. We need to pray that all of these things become more and more our experience.
JESUS BEING THE PERFECTER, THE FINISHER, THE COMPLETER, OF OUR FAITH
Finishing and Completing the Faith That We Need for the Running of the Heavenly Race by Looking Away unto Him Continually
Jesus is the Perfecter, the Finisher, the Completer, of our faith (Heb. 12:2a). As we look away unto Him continually, He will finish and complete the faith that we need for the running of the heavenly race (v. 1). We need to exercise our spirit of faith, our sixth sense, to look away unto Him, not intermittently, but continually.
Jesus Ministering Heaven, Life, and Strength to Us, Transfusing and Infusing Us with All That He Is, That We May Be Able to Run the Heavenly Race and Live the Heavenly Life on Earth
When we look away unto Him, He ministers heaven, life, and strength to us, transfusing and infusing us with all that He is, that we may be able to run the heavenly race and live the heavenly life on earth (2 Cor. 3:18). He ministers heaven into us. Heaven is Christ. When He is infused into us, heaven is infused into us. It is not a small thing to turn our hearts to the Lord. When we say, "Lord, by Your mercy and Your grace, I turn away from everything. I turn my heart to You. I want to just gaze on You. I want to behold You,n this is a great thing! Through such prayers, transactions take place, and our faith is increased.
Having the Same Faith in Quality, but the Quantity of Faith Depending Upon How Much We Contact the Living God to Have Him Increased in Us
We all have the same faith in quality, but the quantity of faith we have depends upon how much we contact the living God so that we may have Him increased in us (Rom. 12:3). To have the quantity of faith increased within us, we need to pray, "Lord, intensify sevenfold my contact with You. Lord, I want to have more and more contact with You." We contact Him by exercising our spirit of faith. We are spirit-exercising people. This means that we are faith-exercising people. By contacting the living God, He is increased within us as faith.
Faith Coming by Contacting the Triune God and Receiving Such Faith by Contacting Its Source, the Lord, the Processed and Consummated God, by Calling on Him, Praying to Him, and Pray-reading His Word
Faith in the progressing stage comes though our contacting the Triune God, who is faith in us (1 Thes. 5:17). The way to receive such a faith is to contact its source, the Lord, the processed and consummated God, by calling on Him, praying to Him, and pray-reading His word (Heb. 4:16; Rom. 10:12; 2 Tim. 2:22; Eph. 6:17-18; Heb. 4:2). Calling on Him is our spiritual breathing. Jeremiah called upon the name of Jehovah from the lowest pit; his calling was his breathing (Lam. 3:55-56). Faith is increased within us whenever we call on the name of the Lord by saying, "O Lord Jesus." We also can pray, "Lord Jesus, I love You. Lord Jesus, I need You. Lord Jesus, I can't make it. But I know You can make it. Lord Jesus, I need You to be my strength." Such simple prayers increase our faith.
Prayer is simply to converse with the Lord, to talk to Him in the most intimate way. If we cannot talk to the Lord, then we cannot talk to anyone. He is our Friend, our Brother, our Husband, our Father, and our Advocate, our Attorney. If the devil bothers us, we can tell him to talk to our Attorney. The Lord is our Comforter, and He is also our Counselor. We can talk to Him about anything. As we talk to Him, we get infused with Him, and He brings us in our conversation with Him to pray for His interests. He infuses us with His desires, and then we pray for His interests.
We also need to pray-read His word. To pray-read the word is to mix the word with faith. Those who fell in the wilderness did not mix the word with faith (Heb. 4:2). To mix the word with faith is to mix the word with our spirit of faith. We can mix the word with faith by the exercise of our spirit of faith to pray-read the word and even to pray over the points which are from the ministry of the word. When we get a verse from the Bible and pray over it by means of all prayer (Eph. 6:17-18), we may repeat it, sing it, declare it, or personalize it. In this way, we are ushered into more experience of Christ.
The Mutuality of Faith by Being Encouraged through the Faith Which Is in One Another
When we contact Him, He is overflowing within us, and there is a mutuality of faith among us; we are encouraged through the faith which is in one another (Rom. 1:12). We are mutually encouraged because of the fellowship of our faith. Speaking to Philemon Paul said, "That the fellowship of your faith may become effective in the full knowledge of every good thing which is in you for Christ" (Philem. 6). The fellowship of our faith stirs up all the good things in the saints for Christ.
Our Regenerated Spirit, Our Spirit of Faith, Being the Victory That Overcomes the Satan-organized-and-usurped World
Our regenerated spirit, our spirit of faith, is the victory that overcomes the Satan-organized-and-usurped world. First John 5:4 says, "For everything that has been begotten of God overcomes the world; and this is the victory which has overcome the world—our faith." Our spirit has been begotten of God. On the one hand, our spirit overcomes the world, and on the other hand, our faith overcomes the world. Our exercised spirit of faith, our sixth sense, which substantiates the unseen, processed, and consummated Triune God, overcomes the Satan-organized-and-usurped world.
The Great Irrepressible and Unlimited Power of Faith Motivating Thousands to Suffer for the Lord, Risk Their Lives, and Become Overcoming Sent Ones and Martyrs for the Carrying Out of God's Eternal Economy
The great irrepressible and unlimited power of faith motivates thousands to suffer for the Lord, risk their lives, and become overcoming sent ones and martyrs for the carrying out of God's eternal economy, which is in faith (Heb. 11:36-40; Luke 18:8; Phil. 2:30; Rom. 16:3-4; Acts 20:24; 1 Tim. 1:4). God's economy is His plan to dispense Himself into man. It is a plan concerning unseen things, substantiated by our spirit of faith. How powerful our spirit of faith is! It motivates us to suffer for the Lord, to risk our lives, to become overcoming sent ones and martyrs for the carrying out of God's eternal economy.
We want to be, by the Lord's mercy, His hidden overcomers who are exercising our spirit of faith to build up the Body of Christ. At the time of Elijah the Lord reserved for Himself seven thousand men who had not bowed the knee to Baal (Rom. 11:4). These are the people of faith, the family of faith, the faith-people. In the note on Hebrews 11:38, Brother Lee said, "These faith-people are an extra-people, a people on the highest plane, of whom the corrupted world is not worthy. Only the holy city of God, New Jerusalem, is worthy to have them." The people who exercise their spirit of faith are extra-people, that is, people on the highest plane. May we be such people.—E. M.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|Outline|对照-目录
　
　
第二十一篇　走信的独一之路以完成神新约的经纶
晨兴-大纲|Outline|对照-听抄-目录
读经：来十一3～十二1；十39
壹　信是享受基督天上职事并经历新遗命中一切遗赠独一的路─来十一1，四2，六1，四3，六12，十22。
贰　亚伯拉罕、摩西、和众殉道者乃是信心历史的一部分，也是独一信心之路的见证人─来十一8～19，23～27，32～38：
一　众信徒的先祖亚伯拉罕因着信，顺从了神的呼召─来十一8，罗四1，12，加三7：
１　亚伯拉罕蒙神呼召时，就因着信出去，并不知道往那里去─来十一8：
a　神向亚伯拉罕显现，呼召他从堕落的光景出来，并将他带回归祂自己这生命树─徒七2。
b　亚伯拉罕的信不是起源于他自己；他之所以相信神，乃是对神的元素传输到他这人里面所起的一种反应。
２　亚伯拉罕因信得称义─创十五6，罗四2～3：
a　亚伯拉罕的信，就是神所传输到他里面的元素，在他里面的涌出─罗四3。
b　神对亚伯拉罕信的反应，乃是称义他，就是算他为义─创十五6。
３　亚伯拉罕经历神是那称无为有的一位，也是那叫死人复活的一位─罗四17：
a　借着以撒的出生，亚伯拉罕经历神是那称无为有的一位─罗四18～21，来十一12。
b　当亚伯拉罕把以撒放在坛上献给神，又得回以撒时，他经历神是那叫死人复活的一位─来十一17～19。
４　亚伯拉罕凭信而活，过祭坛与帐棚的生活─来十一9，创十二7～8：
a　为要凭信而活，我们首先必须筑坛，表征我们在地上的生活是为着神的，然后必须支搭帐棚，指明我们不属于世界。
b　借着过祭坛和帐棚的生活，亚伯拉罕见证他是因着信作寄居的，好象在异地，他羡慕一个更美的家乡，并且等候那座城，其设计者并建筑者乃是神─来十一9～10，16。
二　摩西因着信，拒绝称为法老女儿之子，又选择和神的百姓同受苦害，望断以及于那赏赐，并且坚定不移，如同看见那不能看见的主─来十一24～27。
三　众殉道者因着信，经历神平安的静默，即使神没有作什么来拯救他们，他们仍然运用信心相信神─来十一32～38，太十一6。
叁　信是完成神新约经纶独一的路─提前一4：
一　在基督里的信徒是本于信得称义，也是本于信得生并活着─罗一17，加三11，来十38：
１　信就是那在基督里运行并作工的神，祂将我们放在基督里，使基督成为我们的义（林前一30）；我们凭着这信，才能按着神的公义，合乎法理的得着神圣的生命。─引用经文
２　“义人必本于信得生并活着”（罗一17）─“这话该作为神永远经纶的标语；神的经纶完全是在信里的事（提前一4）！”（罗马书的结晶，一五三页。）─引用经文
二　借着在耶稣基督里的信，我们被带进与基督生机的联结里─加二16：
１　“在耶稣基督里的信”指借着信与基督有生机的联结：
a　这与我们经历基督是那最宝贵者有关，祂注入我们里面，并在我们里面成为我们凭以信入祂的信。
b　信创造一种生机的联结，生命的联结，使我们在其中与基督是一─林前六17。
２　在这生机的联结里，我们经历基督并活基督─弗三17，约十四17，加二20，林后五7：
a　对基督真实的经历是看不见的；这完全是信心的事─来十一1，弗三17。
b　我们不该信靠任何奇特或特异的事；反之，我们该走信心的路，就是读主的话，并信这话─罗十8，17。
三　在基督的身体里，“神…分给各人信心的度量”─罗十二3：
１　神先是在质的一面分给我们信心（彼后一1），然后在量的一面分给我们信心。─引用经文
２　我们的信心有多少，乃在于神的分给。
３　我们应当照着神分给我们的信心，就是神注入到我们里面祂元素的度量，估量自己。
四　信是对得胜者神圣的要求，他们达到这要求，就能在基督凯旋回来时与祂相见，并得着国度的赏赐─路十八8，来十35，39：
１　凭信过得胜生活的信徒，在基督回来时要对祂显为宝贝，预备好得着他们魂的救恩，作他们信心的结果─彼前一7～9，来十39。
２　得胜者要得着基督的赏赐：在千年国里与祂同掌王权，并一同有分于对神圣生命的最高享受─启二十4，6。
３　主要对得胜者说，“好，良善又忠信的奴仆，…进来享受你主人的快乐”─太二五21，23。

TOP-晨兴-大纲|Outline|对照-听抄-目录
　
　
GENERAL SUBJECT
The Crystallization-Study of Epistle to the Hebrews
Message Twenty-One
Taking the Unique Way of Faith to Carry Out God's New Testament Economy
晨兴-纲目|对照-听抄-目录
Scripture Reading: Heb. 11:3—12:1; 10:39
I.　Faith is the unique way to enjoy Christ in His heavenly ministry and to experience all the bequests in the new testament—Heb. 11:1; 4:2; 6:1; 4:3; 6:12; 10:22.
II.　Abraham, Moses, and the martyrs are part of the history of faith and witnesses to the unique way of faith—Heb. 11:8-19, 23-27, 32-38:
A.　By faith Abraham, the forefather of all believers, obeyed God's calling—Heb. 11:8; Rom. 4:1, 12; Gal. 3:7:
1.　When Abraham was called by God, he went out by faith, not knowing where he was going—Heb. 11:8:
a.　God appeared to Abraham to call him out of his fallen condition and to bring him back to Himself as the tree of life—Acts 7:2.
b.　Abraham's faith did not originate with himself; his believing in God was a reaction to the transfusion of God's element into his being.
2.　Abraham was justified by faith—Gen. 15:6; Rom. 4:2-3:
a.　Abraham's believing was the springing up within him of the element that God had transfused into him—Rom. 4:3.
b.　God's reaction to Abraham's believing was to justify him, that is, to account him righteous—Gen. 15:6.
3.　Abraham experienced God as the One who calls things not being as being and as the One who gives life to the dead—Rom. 4:17:
a.　By the birth of Isaac, Abraham experienced God as the One who calls things not being as being—Rom. 4:18-21; Heb. 11:12.
b.　When Abraham received Isaac back after offering him to God on the altar, he experienced God as the One who raises the dead—Heb. 11:17-19.
4.　Abraham lived by faith, living the life of the altar and the tent—Heb. 11:9; Gen. 12:7-8:
a.　In order to live by faith, we must first build an altar, signifying that our life on earth is for God, and then pitch a tent, indicating that we do not belong to the world.
b.　By living the life of the altar and the tent, Abraham testified that he was sojourning by faith, as in a foreign land, longing after a better country and eagerly waiting for the city whose Architect and Builder is God—Heb. 11:9-10, 16.
B.　By faith Moses refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter and chose to be ill treated with the people of God, looking away to the reward and persevering as one seeing the unseen One—Heb. 11:24-27.
C.　By faith the martyrs experienced God's peaceful silence, exercising faith in God even when He did nothing to deliver them—Heb. 11:32-38; Matt. 11:6.
III.　Faith is the unique way to carry out God's New Testament economy—1 Tim. 1:4:
A.　The believers in Christ are justified by faith, and have life and live by faith—Rom. 1:17; Gal. 3:11; Heb. 10:38:
1.　By faith, which is the moving and working God in Christ who puts us into Christ as our righteousness (1 Cor. 1:30), we have the divine life according to God's righteousness judicially.
2.　"The righteous shall have life and live by faith" (Rom. 1:17); "This word should be the banner of God's eternal economy, which is altogether a matter in faith (1 Tim. 1:4)!" (The Crystallization of the Epistle to the Romans,?pp. 123-124).
B.　Through faith in Jesus Christ, we are brought into an organic union with Christ—Gal. 2:16:
1.　Faith in Jesus Christ?denotes an organic union with Christ through believing:
a.　This is related to our experience of Christ as the most precious One, who is infused into us and who becomes in us the faith by which we believe into Him.
b.　Faith creates an organic union, a life union, in which we and Christ are one—1 Cor. 6:17.
2.　In this organic union we experience Christ and live Christ—Eph. 3:17; John 14:17; Gal. 2:20; 2 Cor. 5:7:
a.　The real experience of Christ is invisible; it is altogether a matter of faith—Heb. 11:1; Eph. 3:17.
b.　We should not put our trust in anything extraordinary or spectacular; instead, we should take the way of faith, which is to read the Word and believe it—Rom. 10:8, 17.
C.　In the Body "God has apportioned to each a measure of faith"—Rom. 12:3:
1.　God first allotted faith to us in quality (2 Pet. 1:1), and then He apportioned it in quantity.
2.　How much faith we have depends upon God's apportioning.
3.　We should esteem ourselves according to the faith that God has apportioned to us, that is, according to the measure of God's element that has been infused into us.
D.　Faith is the divine requirement for the overcomers to meet Christ in His triumphant return and to receive the reward of the kingdom—Luke 18:8; Heb. 10:35, 39:
1.　The believers who live an overcoming life by faith will be found by Christ at His return as the treasures ready to receive the salvation of their souls as the end (result) of their faith—1 Pet. 1:7-9; Heb. 10:39.
2.　The overcomers will be rewarded by Christ with the co-kingship and the top enjoyment of divine life with Him in the millennium— Rev. 20:4, 6.
3.　To the overcomers the Lord will say, "Well done, good and faithful slave....Enter into the joy of your master"—Matt. 25:21, 23.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|对照-听抄-目录
　
　
第二十一篇　走信的独一之路以完成神新约的经纶
Taking the Unique Way of Faith to Carry Out God's New Testament Economy
晨兴-纲目|outline-听抄-目录
读经：来十一3～十二1；十39
壹　信是享受基督天上职事并经历新遗命中一切遗赠独一的路─来十一1，四2，六1，四3，六12，十22。
I.　Faith is the unique way to enjoy Christ in His heavenly ministry and to experience all the bequests in the new testament—Heb. 11:1; 4:2; 6:1; 4:3; 6:12; 10:22.
贰　亚伯拉罕、摩西、和众殉道者乃是信心历史的一部分，也是独一信心之路的见证人─来十一8～19，23～27，32～38：
II.　Abraham, Moses, and the martyrs are part of the history of faith and witnesses to the unique way of faith—Heb. 11:8-19, 23-27, 32-38:
一　众信徒的先祖亚伯拉罕因着信，顺从了神的呼召─来十一8，罗四1，12，加三7：
A.　By faith Abraham, the forefather of all believers, obeyed God's calling—Heb. 11:8; Rom. 4:1, 12; Gal. 3:7:
１　亚伯拉罕蒙神呼召时，就因着信出去，并不知道往那里去─来十一8：
1.　When Abraham was called by God, he went out by faith, not knowing where he was going—Heb. 11:8:
a　神向亚伯拉罕显现，呼召他从堕落的光景出来，并将他带回归祂自己这生命树─徒七2。
a.　God appeared to Abraham to call him out of his fallen condition and to bring him back to Himself as the tree of life—Acts 7:2.
b　亚伯拉罕的信不是起源于他自己；他之所以相信神，乃是对神的元素传输到他这人里面所起的一种反应。
b.　Abraham's faith did not originate with himself; his believing in God was a reaction to the transfusion of God's element into his being.
２　亚伯拉罕因信得称义─创十五6，罗四2～3：
2.　Abraham was justified by faith—Gen. 15:6; Rom. 4:2-3:
a　亚伯拉罕的信，就是神所传输到他里面的元素，在他里面的涌出─罗四3。
a.　Abraham's believing was the springing up within him of the element that God had transfused into him—Rom. 4:3.
b　神对亚伯拉罕信的反应，乃是称义他，就是算他为义─创十五6。
b.　God's reaction to Abraham's believing was to justify him, that is, to account him righteous—Gen. 15:6.
３　亚伯拉罕经历神是那称无为有的一位，也是那叫死人复活的一位─罗四17：
3.　Abraham experienced God as the One who calls things not being as being and as the One who gives life to the dead—Rom. 4:17:
a　借着以撒的出生，亚伯拉罕经历神是那称无为有的一位─罗四18～21，来十一12。
a.　By the birth of Isaac, Abraham experienced God as the One who calls things not being as being—Rom. 4:18-21; Heb. 11:12.
b　当亚伯拉罕把以撒放在坛上献给神，又得回以撒时，他经历神是那叫死人复活的一位─来十一17～19。
b.　When Abraham received Isaac back after offering him to God on the altar, he experienced God as the One who raises the dead—Heb. 11:17-19.
４　亚伯拉罕凭信而活，过祭坛与帐棚的生活─来十一9，创十二7～8：
4.　Abraham lived by faith, living the life of the altar and the tent—Heb. 11:9; Gen. 12:7-8:
a　为要凭信而活，我们首先必须筑坛，表征我们在地上的生活是为着神的，然后必须支搭帐棚，指明我们不属于世界。
a.　In order to live by faith, we must first build an altar, signifying that our life on earth is for God, and then pitch a tent, indicating that we do not belong to the world.
b　借着过祭坛和帐棚的生活，亚伯拉罕见证他是因着信作寄居的，好象在异地，他羡慕一个更美的家乡，并且等候那座城，其设计者并建筑者乃是神─来十一9～10，16。
b.　By living the life of the altar and the tent, Abraham testified that he was sojourning by faith, as in a foreign land, longing after a better country and eagerly waiting for the city whose Architect and Builder is God—Heb. 11:9-10, 16.
二　摩西因着信，拒绝称为法老女儿之子，又选择和神的百姓同受苦害，望断以及于那赏赐，并且坚定不移，如同看见那不能看见的主─来十一24～27。
B.　By faith Moses refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter and chose to be ill treated with the people of God, looking away to the reward and persevering as one seeing the unseen One—Heb. 11:24-27.
三　众殉道者因着信，经历神平安的静默，即使神没有作什么来拯救他们，他们仍然运用信心相信神─来十一32～38，太十一6。
C.　By faith the martyrs experienced God's peaceful silence, exercising faith in God even when He did nothing to deliver them—Heb. 11:32-38; Matt. 11:6.
叁　信是完成神新约经纶独一的路─提前一4：
III.　Faith is the unique way to carry out God's New Testament economy—1 Tim. 1:4:
一　在基督里的信徒是本于信得称义，也是本于信得生并活着─罗一17，加三11，来十38：
A.　The believers in Christ are justified by faith, and have life and live by faith—Rom. 1:17; Gal. 3:11; Heb. 10:38:
１　信就是那在基督里运行并作工的神，祂将我们放在基督里，使基督成为我们的义（林前一30）；我们凭着这信，才能按着神的公义，合乎法理的得着神圣的生命。─引用经文
1.　By faith, which is the moving and working God in Christ who puts us into Christ as our righteousness (1 Cor. 1:30), we have the divine life according to God's righteousness judicially.
２　“义人必本于信得生并活着”（罗一17）─“这话该作为神永远经纶的标语；神的经纶完全是在信里的事（提前一4）！”（罗马书的结晶，一五三页。）─引用经文
2.　"The righteous shall have life and live by faith" (Rom. 1:17); "This word should be the banner of God's eternal economy, which is altogether a matter in faith (1 Tim. 1:4)!" (The Crystallization of the Epistle to the Romans,?pp. 123-124).
二　借着在耶稣基督里的信，我们被带进与基督生机的联结里─加二16：
B.　Through faith in Jesus Christ, we are brought into an organic union with Christ—Gal. 2:16:
１　“在耶稣基督里的信”指借着信与基督有生机的联结：
1.　Faith in Jesus Christ?denotes an organic union with Christ through believing:
a　这与我们经历基督是那最宝贵者有关，祂注入我们里面，并在我们里面成为我们凭以信入祂的信。
a.　This is related to our experience of Christ as the most precious One, who is infused into us and who becomes in us the faith by which we believe into Him.
b　信创造一种生机的联结，生命的联结，使我们在其中与基督是一─林前六17。
b.　Faith creates an organic union, a life union, in which we and Christ are one—1 Cor. 6:17.
２　在这生机的联结里，我们经历基督并活基督─弗三17，约十四17，加二20，林后五7：
2.　In this organic union we experience Christ and live Christ—Eph. 3:17; John 14:17; Gal. 2:20; 2 Cor. 5:7:
a　对基督真实的经历是看不见的；这完全是信心的事─来十一1，弗三17。
a.　The real experience of Christ is invisible; it is altogether a matter of faith—Heb. 11:1; Eph. 3:17.
b　我们不该信靠任何奇特或特异的事；反之，我们该走信心的路，就是读主的话，并信这话─罗十8，17。
b.　We should not put our trust in anything extraordinary or spectacular; instead, we should take the way of faith, which is to read the Word and believe it—Rom. 10:8, 17.
三　在基督的身体里，“神…分给各人信心的度量”─罗十二3：
C.　In the Body "God has apportioned to each a measure of faith"—Rom. 12:3:
１　神先是在质的一面分给我们信心（彼后一1），然后在量的一面分给我们信心。─引用经文
1.　God first allotted faith to us in quality (2 Pet. 1:1), and then He apportioned it in quantity.
２　我们的信心有多少，乃在于神的分给。
2.　How much faith we have depends upon God's apportioning.
３　我们应当照着神分给我们的信心，就是神注入到我们里面祂元素的度量，估量自己。
3.　We should esteem ourselves according to the faith that God has apportioned to us, that is, according to the measure of God's element that has been infused into us.
四　信是对得胜者神圣的要求，他们达到这要求，就能在基督凯旋回来时与祂相见，并得着国度的赏赐─路十八8，来十35，39：
D.　Faith is the divine requirement for the overcomers to meet Christ in His triumphant return and to receive the reward of the kingdom—Luke 18:8; Heb. 10:35, 39:
１　凭信过得胜生活的信徒，在基督回来时要对祂显为宝贝，预备好得着他们魂的救恩，作他们信心的结果─彼前一7～9，来十39。
1.　The believers who live an overcoming life by faith will be found by Christ at His return as the treasures ready to receive the salvation of their souls as the end (result) of their faith—1 Pet. 1:7-9; Heb. 10:39.
２　得胜者要得着基督的赏赐：在千年国里与祂同掌王权，并一同有分于对神圣生命的最高享受─启二十4，6。
2.　The overcomers will be rewarded by Christ with the co-kingship and the top enjoyment of divine life with Him in the millennium— Rev. 20:4, 6.
３　主要对得胜者说，“好，良善又忠信的奴仆，…进来享受你主人的快乐”─太二五21，23。
3.　To the overcomers the Lord will say, "Well done, good and faithful slave....Enter into the joy of your master"—Matt. 25:21, 23.

TOP-晨兴-纲目|outline-听抄-目录
　
　
MESSAGE TWENTY-ONE
TAKING THE UNIQUE WAY OF FAITH TO CARRY OUT GOD'S NEW TESTAMENT ECONOMY
晨兴-纲目|Outline|对照-目录
文档OCR所得，仅供追求参考
Prayer: Lord Jesus, we really need You as all the offerings. We have so many needs and situations that require You to be the offerings for us in a real way at this very moment. Lord, we praise and thank You that You were made sin for us. You, who knew no sin, were made sin for us that we might become the righteousness of God. Lord, we thank You that You are the unlimited trespass offering, bearing all of our sins upon the cross. Lord, we especially need You now as the whole burnt offering. You were and are absolutely for God. Lord, we need to touch You as the meal offering to be a proper human being according to God. Lord Jesus, You are the peace offering. Thank You that You made peace on the cross. You brought peace to us through the gospel, and now You are the God of peace and the peace of God in our mingled spirit. Lord, with You as all these offerings and with faith in Your precious blood, we come boldly into the Holy of Holies to meet with God and to receive Your Word and life supply. Amen.
In the previous message we received a clear, faithful, substantial, and practical word concerning Christ as the Author and Perfecter of our faith. God knows that we do not have the proper ability to believe. Human beings can believe things, but that kind of belief may not be a proper and genuine believing in the living God. People may be subject to all manner of superstitious and fanciful things due to the natural credulity that is in fallen humanity. This kind of believing has no place in God's economy. The genuine faith is a divine believing ability. Faith is Christ Himself infused into us by God as we turn away from everything and behold the Lord and appreciate the Lord. We are not believing cleverly devised myths (2 Pet. 1:16), but we believe the divine reality, the common faith, God's New Testament economy, which has been revealed to us. We believe with a divine believing ability imparted into us by God.
In my youth and inexperience I thought that if I had some kind of need, financial or otherwise, everything would be so simple. I thought that all I needed to do was to believe God, because He is omnipotent, omnipresent, and knows everything. However, I discovered after many experiences that when there was no money or food in the house, neither was there any faith. I then began to lose trust in myself to have any natural believing ability. I hope the day will come when you realize that you can only believe God by God as your believing ability.
God does not expect or want us to generate some kind of faith out of our own heart. Rather, He would speak faith into us through the word because faith comes out of hearing and hearing through the word of Christ (Rom. 10:17). Our God who is the real giant of faith and the Father of faith will transfuse His believing element into us little by little until there is a reaction and a response in us- that response is the beginning of faith. Christ truly is the Author of faith (Heb. 12:2). We need to look away unto Him, behold Him, open to Him, and allow His glorious believing ability to be transfused into us. Do not try to feel faith coming in or try to sense it; just let Him shine into your being.
When God as faith is transfused into us, we believe. Once we start believing, we go through a lifelong process of having our faith perfected, completed, and trained stage by stage. We all have had a beginning under the Author of faith, yet we should be willing to realize that there is a lot ahead of us. There is quite a journey and race ahead of us, which will require in its various stages greater and greater faith. It took some faith for Abraham to leave Ur of the Chaldees. It took some faith for Abraham to believe God and to be justified. It took more faith for him to believe that Isaac would come forth, and it took much more faith for him to obey God and offer Isaac back to God as a burnt offering.
We all had our beginning in the pagan land of idolatry, Ur of the Chaldees. But we must all go on until we join our forefathers on the top of Mount Moriah to offer Christ as the burnt offering to God for His satisfaction that God's heart's desire may be fulfilled and His eternal purpose may be accomplished. We should allow the Lord to shepherd and minister to us at whatever stage we are in.
FAITH BEING THE UNIQUE WAY TO ENJOY CHRIST IN HIS HEAVENLY MINISTRY AND TO EXPERIENCE ALL THE BEQUESTS IN THE NEW TESTAMENT
Faith is the unique way to enjoy Christ in His heavenly ministry and to experience all the bequests in the new testament (Heb. 11:1; 4:2; 6:1; 4:3; 6:12; 10:22). Some saints may wish that the Lord would be tangible. However, this is not the way of faith. It is instead an attempt to bring into the physical realm the things that are in the divine and mystical realm. We cannot feel God by Our emotion directly, we cannot touch Him by our mind directly, and we cannot will ourselves into His presence. Faith is the unique way to enjoy Christ in His heavenly ministry and to experience all the bequests of the new testament. The Lord is real as the Spirit of reality. He is real by faith.
Faith is versus the law. The law is a matter of self-effort and of trying to use our natural strength. Eventually, we will realize that everything we produce by our natural ability will become a rejected "Ishmael." Abraham had to cast out Ishmael because Ishmael was not the issue of faith (Gen. 21:10; Gal. 4:30).
ABRAHAM, MOSES, AND THE MARTYRS BEING WITNESSES TO THE UNIQUE WAY OF FAITH
Abraham, Moses, and the martyrs are part of the history of faith and witnesses to the unique way of faith (Heb. 11:8-19, 23-27, 32-38). Some witnesses have gone before us. They were born in sin just as we were and they needed redemption just as we did. They needed God infused into them just as we do. Yet their experiences, particularly those of Abraham, provide a "pneumatic map" to point the way for us. As the sons of Abraham and the sons of God through faith, we are walking in the steps of the faith of our father Abraham (Rom. 4:12), from Ur of the Chaldees to Mount Moriah.
Abraham, the Forefather of All Believers, Obeying God's Calling by Faith By faith
Abraham, the forefather of all believers, obeyed God's calling (Heb. 11:8; Rom. 4:1, 12; Gal. 3:7). The book of Romans mentions the obedience of faith (1:5; 16:26). If there is faith, there will be some kind of response in a concrete action of obedience. To take the way of faith is to take an actual way of living and walking in all kinds of situations with many kinds of concrete responses as acts of obedience. For example, if the God of glory begins to shine on you and you get some feeling that you should migrate, the Lord knows you may not have the faith or initially even the willingness to migrate. His first aim is to get you to open, to subdue your will, and to disarm you of every excuse and argument.
Abraham did not sit forever getting infused, having a sweet time, and never having to do or say anything or go anywhere. If that had been the case, none of us would be here. Abraham obeyed. We also are "Abrahams," An Abraham is first someone who has been called out. Second, an Abraham is a person who no longer lives and walks by himself. Faith means that we reject ourselves. It is the experience of "no longer I...but...Christ", (Gal. 2:20). We do not live and walk by ourselves. Third, an Abraham is someone who forsakes everything he has by nature until the only thing that remains is God. With Abraham, not even what was given by God, such as Isaac, could remain instead of God. God does not want us to be directly related to anything or anyone that would replace our absolute dependence on and moment-by- moment contact with Him.
Faith means that you reject and renounce yourself. Faith means that you have no trust in what you can do in your natural ability. We are in a training to reject ourselves and to allow the Lord to bring us into the realm of resurrection life. We hope that after the full-time training the trainees would just simply follow the Lamb wherever He goes. We desire that they follow the God of Abraham step by step as a pilgrim and a sojourner, setting up an altar and a tent to openly testify, am for God, not for myself. I do not belong to this earth or to this age. I am a citizen of another country. I am looking for another city. This world is not worthy of me. I am a sojourner and a pilgrim until I meet the Lord."
Abraham Going Out by Faith, Not Knowing Where He Was Going
When Abraham was called by God, he went out by faith, not knowing where he was going (Heb. 11:8). Not knowing means that it is an adventure. God appeared to Abraham to call him out of his fallen condition and to bring him back to Himself as the tree of life (Acts 7:2). The God of glory appeared to Abraham and shined and radiated Himself into him until there was a response to bring him back to Himself as the tree of life. The principle of the tree of life is the principle of dependence, of not knowing.
Abraham's faith did not originate with himself; his believing in God was a reaction to the transfusion of God's element into his being. We should not try to stir up our faith. The Bible speaks of unfeigned faith (1 Tim. 1:5), meaning faith without hypocrisy. This implies that there can be a counterfeit, hypocritical, and self-made faith. We are not here to fake it or make it, but to have faith produced in us by the Lord's shining. Eventually, there will be a reaction and a response even from the most stiff, formal, unspontaneous, slow, cautious, careful, deliberate, thoughtful, fearful, timid, self-conscious souls. The God of glory is able to elicit a response. He just wants us to be open to Him to receive the transfusion.
Abraham Being Justified by Faith
Abraham was justified by faith (Gen. 15:6; Rom, 4:2-3), To be justified means to be approved by God according to the standard of His righteousness. No one can make himself approved to God by the works of the law or by self-effort. God has made Christ our righteousness.
When we believe this through the infusion of faith, we react. As soon as we react, God reacts to our reaction. When we say, "I believe/ He says, "Justified." When we say, "I believe," He says, "Forgiven!" When we say, "I believe,w He says, a I forget your sins." There is a marvelous interaction up and down the heavenly ladder. The Lord infuses faith and we respond. Initially, we receive enough faith to be justified and to be born again. Later, the "bar" goes higher and the price is greater. The unique way is the way of faith. He is not only the Author but also the Perfecter.
The enemy may tempt us to look at ourselves, but our response should be, "Enemy, look with me to the glorified God-man on the throne in the heavens. He is wonderful!Peter, at the word of the Lord, got out of the boat and walked on the water (Matt. 14:29). But when he changed his gaze and began to consider the water, the waves, and the wind, he began to sink (v. 30). The person walking on the waves was the God-man. The one sleeping in the boat was the God-man. We may pray desperate prayers for deliverance, but He may be asleep. He is not in the realm of the visible. The Lord had told the disciples to go to the other side (v. 22). That word was a guarantee that they would reach the other side. Often we are those with little faith. Faith is realizing who Jesus is. We need to look away and realize who Jesus is.
Abraham's believing was the springing up within him of the element that God had transfused into him (Rom. 4:3). We should not give up on our gospel friends so easily. We should continue to speak faith into them. We also should go within the veil to get a fresh transfusion and then shine on them. They may not want us to come, yet they like us because we shine on them. Do not give up on your parents, your relatives, and your friends. Believe in what can happen when the God of glory shines into people. Faith will spring up within them. God's reaction to Abraham's believing was to justify him, that is, to account him righteous (Gen. 15:6).
Experiencing God as the One Who Calls Things Not Being as Being and the One Who Gives Life to the Dead
Abraham experienced God as the One who calls things not being as being and as the One who gives life to the dead (Rom. 4:17). This world is a constitution of nothingness and death. Abraham had to experience God in two major stages: first as the God who calls things not being as being, and second as the God who gives life to the dead.
Abraham Experiencing God as the One Who Calls Things Not Being as Being by the Birth of Isaac
By the birth of Isaac, Abraham experienced God as the One who calls things not being as being (Rom. 4:18-21; Heb. 11:12). God promised Abraham an heir, yet nothing happened. Sarah then came up with a proposal, which Abraham acted upon (Gen. 16:2). Later, Sarah blamed him for acting on her advice (v. 5). God also was not happy, for He did not speak to Abraham for thirteen years (v. 16; 17:1). God did not do anything outwardly to punish him; He just remained silent. His silence makes us very aware of His presence. His absence creates in us a longing for His speaking. After a time, we will say, "Lord, speak to me. Give me a word. Do not leave me without a word. I repent of acting out of myself, of doing Your will in my way, in my time, by my flesh. I accept the circumcision. I agree with it. I do not pity myself. I say amen to You. Kill my capacity to generate 'Ishmaels' from the flesh."
Eventually, Abraham reached the age of one hundred and Sarah the age of ninety (Gen. 17:17). At that time, God visited them. Genesis 18:10-15 says:
And he said, I will certainly return unto thee according to the time of life; and, lo, Sarah thy wife shall have a son....Therefore Sarah laughed within herself, saying, After I am waxed old shall I have pleasure, my lord being old also? And the Lord said unto Abraham, Wherefore did Sarah laugh, saying, Shall I of a surety bear a child, which am old? Is any thing too hard for the Lord? At the time appointed I will return unto thee, according to the time of life, and Sarah shall have a son. Then Sarah denied, saying, I laughed not; for she was afraid. And he said, Nay; but thou didst laugh.
A year after this appearing by God, Isaac was indeed born. Then Abraham knew God as the God who calls5 things not being as being. There was no Isaac, and it seemed to be impossible to have Isaac. But God did the impossible. The things that are impossible with man are possible with God (Matt. 19:26; Luke 1:37). This is a precious experience.
Abraham Experiencing God as the One Who Raises the Dead by Receiving Isaac Back after Offering Him to God
When Abraham received Isaac back after offering him to God on the altar, he experienced God as the One who raises the dead (Heb. 11:17-19), Abraham finally had Isaac by faith and enjoyed him for a long period of time, especially in Beer-sheba. He bought a well there with seven ewe lambs and named the well Beer-sheba, which means well of the covenant, of an oath, signifying the covenanted water, based upon the redemption of Christ (Gen. 21:28-31). Abraham enjoyed the Lord as a tamarisk tree, as the tree of life, and he called on God as Jehovah El Olam, which means the eternal God (v. 33), Then one day God spoke to him, saying, "Take now thy son, thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest, and get thee into the land of Moriah; and offer him there for a burnt offering upon one of the mountains which I will tell thee of" (22:2). Abraham went, living in what we may from a human viewpoint call a divine contradiction. Isaac was the promised seed, and everything depended on what would come out of Isaac. Now he was to be killed. However, Abraham did not reason; he just obeyed.
We should not hold on to the things that are from God, even something precious of Christ or in Christ. God is still the unique source. Do not allow Isaac to become the replacement for God the Father. Isaac may have met our need, but God's heart is still longing. His need is not met until Isaac is offered and multiplied. Abraham's relationship with Isaac had to be severed and brought into resurrection. Therefore, Abraham was told to offer Isaac as a burnt offering for God's satisfaction.
We should not play a "Mount Moriah game" with God. We may think that God is just testing us to see if we are willing to do it, and that once we are willing to do it, God will not require us to do it. This is not the case. In God's sight Isaac was killed. Christ, the real Isaac, was put to death. According to Hebrews 11, Abraham had the faith to receive Isaac back in resurrection, and in figure he did. What we put on the altar on Mount Moriah is actually put to death. It is not a game. It is a costly transaction. It is a burnt offering that is ascending to God in Christ for His satisfaction.
At this stage we know God not only as the unique source out from whom all things come, but also as the unique goal unto whom are all things. Romans 11:36 says, "Because out from Him and through Him and to Him are all things. To Him be the glory forever. Amen." We may have believed for something to come into our life out from Him. But the question is whether or not we will allow that thing which is from Him to be unto Him. We should be able to stand in His presence and say, Everything is unto You. I am a ceaseless cycle of dispensing and offering up. I hold on to nothing, not to the highest, the best, or the dearest that You have given me." Nothing replaces Him. Only He is God the Father, the unique source and unique goal. We will all go unto Him. Everything must go unto Him.
When Abraham was about to ascend to the mountain, he told his servants that he and Isaac were going to worship (Gen. 22:5). Abraham experienced God as the God of resurrection, the God who raises the dead. This is so crucial because in the fulfillment of the type it was through this process that the only begotten Son became the Firstborn among many brothers. God the Father so loved the world that He gave His Only Begotten (John 3:16). This was not just a test. There was a real Mount Moriah, and the only begotten Son died there in His humanity, and God received Him back in resurrection as the Firstborn. On the day of resurrection He could wave a sheaf of the firstfruits, the many sons. He could testify, Father, I left You alone, but I come back with a great multiplication. I am the Firstborn in My humanity but still the only begotten Son in My deity. Now I have many brothers." God's heart is satisfied because He now has the multiplication of Christ through this transaction of faith.
Moriah means the vision of Jah, the vision of Jehovah. When we are in
the God of resurrection enjoying Him as Jehovah Jireh, which means the Lord will provide, we receive a provision. The issue of that provision is a crystal-clear vision. The place where Isaac was offered became the location of the threshing floor of Araunah, which David purchased (2 Sam. 24:16-25). That location became Zion, where the temple was built. Abraham pioneered the way for all of his sons in faith to go to Moriah and to end up on Mount Zion. After Abraham's offering, the Lord said, ''Because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son, that in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the seashore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies" (Gen. 22:16-17), This is the kingdom, which is in the church as the Body of Christ. Because Abraham offered Isaac, because the Father offered the Only Begotten, there is a seed that is the multiplication of Christ, and there is the church, the Body, and the kingdom.
The Lord's recovery needs a tremendous release of resurrection life. The blessing God wants to give is bestowed on Mount Moriah in resurrection. We cannot and should not try to ascend Mount Moriah by an act of our will, but as we are enjoying the church life in Beer-sheba, calling on the Lord, enjoying the tree of life, and drinking of the well, eventually something will be built up in our being. Then there will be a deep turn in secret before the Father to begin a three-day journey to Moriah to offer up all we are and have in Christ. Everything we have received from God- His calling, His commission, His w o rk w e present back to Him as a response in faith. It goes into death and comes forth in resurrection with multiplication, resulting in God being worshipped and us being blessed. Then Isaac is returned in resurrection. We need such fathers in the church who would take this journey of faith.
Abraham Living by Faith the Life of the Altar and the Tent
Abraham lived by faith, living the life of the altar and the tent (Heb. 11:9; Gen. 12:7-8). In order to live by faith, we must first build an altar, signifying that our life on earth is for God, and then pitch a tent, indicating that we do not belong to the world. By living the life of the altar and the tent, Abraham testified that he was sojourning by faith, as in a foreign land, longing after a better country and eagerly waiting for the city whose Architect and Builder is God (Heb. 11:9-10, 16).
By Faith Moses Refusing to Be Called the Son of Pharaoh's Daughter and Choosing to Be Ill Treated with the People of God, Looked Away to the Reward and Persevered as One Having Seen the Unseen One
By faith Moses refused to be called the son of Pharaohs daughter and chose to be ill treated with the people of God, looking away to the reward and persevering as one seeing the unseen One (Heb. 11:24-27). I believe that Moses7 mother prayed for him during the few precious months or years when she could nurse him for Pharaoh's daughter. Perhaps she prayed that Moses would realize when he became an adult that he was not an Egyptian, but a Hebrew, a river crosser. Moses was able to say, "I turn my back on the palace in Egypt. I do. not fear the wrath of the king. I choose to suffer affliction with the people of God because I am not an Egyptian. I am a Hebrew? I believe the heavenly Christ as the life-giving Spirit will touch many young believers who may be in Egypt. Something within them will rise up to say, "I am not an Egyptian. I am not ashamed to be a believer? We must go to the incense altar and pray for them to come back to the suffering people of God where they belong. Moses endured, having seen the unseen One. He made a choice. He refused to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter.
After this, Moses went through a period of a full-time training." For forty years he lived in another kind of divine contradiction. He tended the flock of Jethro his father-in-law until everything of the natural life died out. Then unexpectedly he saw the burning bush and was called to lead the exodus of God's chosen people out of Egypt into Canaan (Exo. 3:1-10), out of the world into God's building.
By Faith the Martyrs Experienced God's Peaceful Silence
By faith the martyrs experienced God's peaceful silence, exercising faith in God even when He did nothing to deliver them (Heb. 11:32-38), The Lord's word to John the Baptist in prison was, 'And blessed is he who is not stumbled because of Men (Matt. 11:6). Blessed is the person who can believe God in spite of what He does not do, in spite of the prayers that do not seem to be heard, and in spite of no action to make our life easier.
After speaking a glorious prophecy, Isaiah may have been sawn in two. There was neither intervention nor a miracle. Even if God would not deliver us from the fiery furnace, as He did with Daniels friends (Dan. 3:16-28), we would not worship the golden idol. If He does something, He is our God, If He does nothing, He is still our God. Our God is everything as the reality of the new covenant. Let Him do as it seems good to Him.
FAITH BEING THE UNIQUE WAY TO CARRY OUT GOD'S ECONOMY
The Believers in Christ Being Justified by Faith and Having Life and Living by Faith
Faith is the unique way to carry out God's New Testament economy (1 Tim. 1:4). The believers in Christ are justified by faith, and have life and live by faith (Rom. 1:17; Gal. 3:11; Heb. 10:38). By faith, which is the moving and working of God who puts us into Christ as our righteousness (1 Cor. 1:30), we have the divine life according to God's righteousness judicially. In The Crystallization of the Epistle to the Romans, pages 123-124, Brother Lee says, "'The righteous shall have life and live by faith)\ [Rom. 1:17]. This word should be the banner of God's eternal economy, which is altogether a matter in faith (1 Tim. 1:4)!" We became righteous by faith, and now we live by faith.
The Believers through Faith Being Brought into an Organic Union with Christ
Through faith in Jesus Christ, we are brought into an organic union with Christ (Gal. 2:16). Faith in Jesus Christ denotes an organic union with Christ through believing. This is related to our experience of Christ as the most precious One, who is infused into us and who becomes in us the faith by which we believe into Him.
